message.content:
We're building a state of the art sandbox implementation for our extensions and plugins. We want to support 4 platforms: linux x86_64, linux arm64, macos x86_64, mscos arm64, with a caveat: macos 86_64 is not a priority so might drop it. There's a project at github.com/jinkkaihe/matchlock that looks promising, there's also github.com/apple/container as well, which we could combine with just one of the million container virtualization frameworks for linux. Agent Sandbox Security: Essential Features
This is a great topic, especially given where you are with Rune and the broader shift toward agentic tooling. Here's a comprehensive breakdown of what makes an agent sandbox effective at protecting the user.

Process Isolation
The foundation of any sandbox is strong process isolation. The agent's execution environment should be separated from the host system using one or more of these mechanisms:
Namespace isolation — The agent process should operate in its own PID, network, mount, user, and IPC namespaces. This prevents it from seeing or interacting with host processes, network interfaces, or filesystems it shouldn't touch. On Linux, this means leveraging clone() with appropriate CLONE_NEW* flags or using higher-level abstractions like containers.
Filesystem confinement — The agent should have a restricted view of the filesystem, ideally a chroot or overlay mount that exposes only what it needs. Writes should be captured in an ephemeral layer (copy-on-write) so the host filesystem is never mutated directly. Any changes can then be reviewed and selectively applied.
Seccomp and capability dropping — Even within a namespace, the syscall surface should be minimized. A seccomp-bpf profile that whitelists only necessary syscalls dramatically reduces the blast radius of any exploit. All Linux capabilities should be dropped except those explicitly needed.

Resource Controls
An agent that can exhaust CPU, memory, disk, or network is a denial-of-service vector against the user's own machine.
Cgroup limits — Hard ceilings on CPU time (both wall-clock and CPU-seconds), memory (with OOM kill rather than swap thrashing), PIDs (to prevent fork bombs), and I/O bandwidth. These should be non-negotiable defaults, not opt-in.
Execution timeouts — Every agent action should have a wall-clock timeout. If an agent hangs or enters an infinite loop, the sandbox should kill it deterministically. This needs to be layered: per-command timeout, per-task timeout, and a global session timeout.
Disk quotas — The ephemeral filesystem layer should have a hard size cap. An agent that fills a disk can destabilize the entire system.

Network Policy
Network access is one of the highest-risk capabilities you can give an agent.
Default-deny egress — The agent should have no network access by default. If network is needed, it should go through a proxy that enforces an explicit allowlist of domains/IPs and protocols.
No listening sockets — The agent should never be able to bind and listen on ports accessible from the host or LAN, preventing it from opening backdoors.
DNS filtering — Even with an allowlist, DNS queries should be intercepted and filtered to prevent DNS-based exfiltration or tunneling.
Rate limiting — Outbound connections should be rate-limited to prevent data exfiltration at scale even through allowed channels.

Permission and Capability Model
This is where most sandboxes fall short — they give binary access (sandbox on/off) instead of a granular capability model.
Declarative capability requests — Before execution, the agent should declare what it needs: filesystem paths (read/write/execute), network endpoints, environment variables, subprocess spawning, etc. The user reviews and approves a capability set before the agent runs.
Least privilege by default — If the agent doesn't request a capability, it doesn't get it. Unknown capabilities are denied, not granted.
Tiered approval — Low-risk actions (reading files in a project directory) can be auto-approved. Medium-risk actions (writing files, running build commands) get a one-time approval or session-scoped approval. High-risk actions (network access, running arbitrary binaries, modifying dotfiles) require explicit per-invocation approval.
Revocable permissions — The user should be able to revoke a granted permission mid-session. The sandbox must be able to enforce this immediately, not just on the next action.

Action Audit and Transparency
The user should never be in the dark about what the agent is doing.
Complete action log — Every syscall, file operation, network request, and subprocess invocation should be logged in a structured, queryable format. This is the agent's equivalent of an audit trail.
Real-time visibility — The user should be able to see a live feed of what the agent is doing, not just the final output. This includes commands being run, files being modified, and network requests being made.
Diff-based review — For filesystem mutations, the sandbox should present changes as diffs (like a git staging area) that the user can accept, reject, or modify before they're applied to the real filesystem. This is the single most important UX feature for trust.
Reproducibility — The action log should be detailed enough that the entire session can be replayed or audited after the fact.

Rollback and Recovery
Even with all protections, things go wrong. The sandbox must make recovery trivial.
Snapshot-based state management — The sandbox should snapshot the relevant state (filesystem, environment) before the agent begins. If anything goes wrong, one-click rollback to the pre-agent state.
Incremental checkpoints — For long-running agent tasks, periodic checkpoints allow partial rollback without losing all progress.
Transaction semantics — Ideally, agent actions are only committed to the real environment when explicitly approved. Until then, everything lives in a transactional overlay.

Secret and Credential Protection
Agents operating in a developer's environment are surrounded by sensitive material.
Environment scrubbing — The agent's environment should not inherit the user's shell environment by default. API keys, tokens, credentials in ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.config, etc. should be invisible to the agent unless explicitly passed through.
Credential injection, not exposure — If the agent needs to authenticate somewhere, credentials should be injected at the point of use (e.g., a proxy that adds auth headers) rather than exposed to the agent process. The agent never sees the raw secret.
Clipboard isolation — The agent should not be able to read the system clipboard, which often contains sensitive data.
Memory protection — After the sandbox session ends, all agent memory should be zeroed. No core dumps should be writable from within the sandbox.

Input/Output Sanitization
The boundary between the agent and the host is a trust boundary.
Output validation — Files produced by the agent should be scanned or validated before being applied. This includes checking for embedded scripts in documents, symlink attacks, or path traversal in generated filenames.
Prompt injection resistance — If the agent reads files or network responses as part of its context, the sandbox should flag or sanitize content that looks like prompt injection attempts. This is an emerging and genuinely hard problem, but at minimum the architecture should separate "data" from "instructions" as clearly as possible.
Symlink and hardlink restrictions — The agent should not be able to create symlinks or hardlinks that point outside its confined filesystem. This is a classic container escape vector.

Escape and Escalation Prevention
The sandbox itself must be hardened against breakout.
No ptrace — The agent should not be able to attach to other processes. PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS should be set.
Read-only root filesystem — The sandbox's own runtime (not the working overlay) should be immutable.
Minimal attack surface — The sandbox environment should contain the absolute minimum set of binaries and libraries. No compilers, debuggers, or network tools unless explicitly needed and approved.
Nested execution prevention — The agent should not be able to launch its own sandbox or container runtime, which could be used to escape the outer sandbox.

content.thinking:
The user is asking me to help them build a sandbox implementation for their Rune IDE extensions and plugins. They want to support multiple platforms (Linux x86_64, Linux arm64, macOS x86_64, macOS arm64), with macOS x86_64 being lower priority.

They've mentioned two projects:
1. github.com/jinkkaihe/matchlock - looks promising
2. github.com/apple/container - for macOS, could combine with container virtualization for Linux

They've also provided a comprehensive list of features they want in the sandbox:
- Process isolation (namespaces, filesystem confinement, seccomp, capabilities)
- Resource controls (cgroups, timeouts, disk quotas)
- Network policy (default-deny egress, no listening sockets, DNS filtering, rate limiting)
- Permission and capability model (declarative requests, least privilege, tiered approval, revocable)
- Action audit and transparency (logging, real-time visibility, diff-based review, reproducibility)
- Rollback and recovery (snapshots, checkpoints, transaction semantics)
- Secret and credential protection (environment scrubbing, credential injection, clipboard isolation, memory protection)
- Input/output sanitization (output validation, prompt injection resistance, symlink restrictions)
- Escape and escalation prevention (no ptrace, read-only root, minimal attack surface, nested execution prevention)

This is a substantial project. Let me think about how to approach this:

1. First, I should research the two projects they mentioned to understand what they offer
2. Then, I should look at their existing codebase structure to understand how sandbox would fit in
3. Finally, I should help them plan/implement the sandbox

Given the complexity of this task, I think entering plan mode would be appropriate. This is a significant architectural decision that will touch many aspects of their system.

Let me first explore their existing codebase to understand the context better, and also research the mentioned projects.

content.text: This is a substantial project - building a production-grade sandbox with process isolation, resource controls, network policy, capability models, and more across multiple platforms. Let me start by researching the projects you mentioned and exploring your current codebase structure.

input.prompt:
Research the project at github.com/jinkkaihe/matchlock (or similar variations like jinkkaiheiern/matchlock, etc.). I need to understand:

1. What this project does - is it a sandbox/container runtime?
2. What platforms does it support?
3. What isolation mechanisms does it use (namespaces, seccomp, cgroups, etc.)?
4. Is it written in Go?
5. How mature is it? (stars, recent commits, contributors)
6. What's the API/interface like?

Use web search and fetch the GitHub repo to gather this information. If the exact URL doesn't work, search for "matchlock sandbox" or "matchlock container" on GitHub.

     2→//

     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package extensionapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"bufio"
    19→	"context"
    20→	"encoding/json"
    21→	"errors"
    22→	"fmt"
    23→	"io"
    24→	"log/slog"
    25→	"os"
    26→	"os/signal"
    27→	"syscall"
    28→	"time"
    29→
    30→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config"
    31→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/debug"
    32→	"golang.org/x/oauth2"
    33→)
    34→
    35→// Metadata represents the developer and extension metadata sent by the extension
    36→// over stdout to the extension host.
    37→type Metadata struct {
    38→	DeveloperID      string      `json:"developer_id"`
    39→	DeveloperEmail   string      `json:"developer_email"`
    40→	DeveloperKey     string      `json:"developer_key"`
    41→	ExtensionID      string      `json:"id"`
    42→	ExtensionName    string      `json:"name"`
    43→	ExtensionVersion string      `json:"version"`
    44→	Permissions      Permissions `json:"permissions"`
    45→}
    46→
    47→// WorkspaceExtension abstracts ExtendWorkspace, which needs to
    48→// be satisfied by extensions passed to ServeWorkspaceExtension.
    49→type WorkspaceExtension interface {
    50→	ExtendWorkspace(context.Context, *Workspace, config.Config) error
    51→}
    52→
    53→// ServeWorkspaceExtension serves the given workspace extension with the given
    54→// developer and extension metadata. This function blocks until a signal is received
    55→// for the extension to shutdown, in which case the returned error is nil,
    56→// or another error occurs.
    57→func ServeWorkspaceExtension(extension WorkspaceExtension, meta Metadata) error {
    58→	return serveWorkspaceExtension(extension, meta, os.Stdin, os.Stdout)
    59→}
    60→
    61→// Config is sent by the host to the extension over stdin.
    62→// It contains the connection configuration needed to establish
    63→// gRPC connections and access workspace resources.
    64→type Config struct {
    65→	// Socket is the unix socket used to establish
    66→	// a secure communication channel with host.
    67→	Socket string `json:"socket"`
    68→	// Token is the oauth2 token used to authenticate
    69→	// and authorize requests against workspace resources.
    70→	Token *oauth2.Token `json:"token"`
    71→	// Certificate is the certificate used to secure the connections.
    72→	Certificate []byte `json:"certificate"`
    73→	// Config is the user's configuration for the running extension.
    74→	Config  map[string]any `json:"config"`
    75→	DataDir string         `json:"datadir"`
    76→}
    77→
    78→// FuncWorkspaceExtension returns a WorkspaceExtension that calls fn
    79→// on calls to ExtendWorkspace.
    80→func FuncWorkspaceExtension(
    81→	fn func(context.Context, *Workspace, config.Config) error,
    82→) WorkspaceExtension {
    83→	return fnWorkspaceExtension{fn: fn}
    84→}
    85→
    86→func serveWorkspaceExtension(
    87→	extension WorkspaceExtension, meta Metadata,
    88→	in io.ReadCloser, out io.Writer,
    89→) error {
    90→	sigchan := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
    91→	signal.Notify(sigchan, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
    92→	signal.Ignore(syscall.SIGPIPE)
    93→	defer signal.Stop(sigchan)
    94→
    95→	level := getLogLevelEnv()
    96→	setupExtensionLogging(level)
    97→
    98→	data, err := json.Marshal(meta)
    99→	if err != nil {
   100→		return fmt.Errorf("marshal protocol response: %w", err)
   101→	}
   102→
   103→	if _, err := out.Write(data); err != nil {
   104→		return fmt.Errorf("write protocol response: %w", err)
   105→	}
   106→	_ = os.Stdout.Sync()
   107→
   108→	scanner := bufio.NewScanner(in)
   109→	ok := scanner.Scan()
   110→	if !ok {
   111→		return fmt.Errorf("scan protocol exchange: %w", scanner.Err())
   112→	}
   113→
   114→	var req Config
   115→	if err := json.Unmarshal(scanner.Bytes(), &req); err != nil {
   116→		return fmt.Errorf("unmarshal protocol request: %w", err)
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	if req.Socket == "" {
   120→		return errors.New("protocol exchange error: socket is missing" +
   121→			" in request")
   122→	}
   123→
   124→	start := time.Now()
   125→	errchan := make(chan error, 1)
   126→	cfg := config.MapConfig(req.Config)
   127→	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
   128→	defer cancel()
   129→
   130→	go debug.CapturePanicReport(func() {
   131→		workspace, err := NewWorkspace(req, meta)
   132→		if err != nil {
   133→			errchan <- err
   134→			return
   135→		}
   136→		panicValue, captureErr, ok := debug.CapturePanicReportWith(req.DataDir,
   137→			meta.ExtensionID, meta.ExtensionVersion, func() {
   138→				errchan <- extension.ExtendWorkspace(ctx, workspace, cfg)
   139→			})
   140→		if ok {
   141→			return
   142→		}
   143→		if captureErr != nil {
   144→			panic(fmt.Sprintf("capture panic report: "+
   145→				"error capturing: %v: original panic: %v", captureErr, panicValue))
   146→		}
   147→		panicErr := fmt.Errorf("extension panic: %v", panicValue)
   148→		// ensure log is delivered
   149→		_ = os.Stderr.Sync()
   150→		errchan <- panicErr
   151→	})
   152→
   153→	select {
   154→	case err := <-errchan:
   155→		if err != nil {
   156→			slog.Error("extension is exiting", "error", err)
   157→			return err
   158→		}
   159→		slog.Debug("extension was setup correctly", "duration", time.Since(start))
   160→		<-sigchan
   161→		return nil
   162→	case <-sigchan:
   163→		return nil
   164→	}
   165→}
   166→
   167→type fnWorkspaceExtension struct {
   168→	fn func(context.Context, *Workspace, config.Config) error
   169→}
   170→
   171→func (fn fnWorkspaceExtension) ExtendWorkspace(
   172→	ctx context.Context, w *Workspace, cfg config.Config,
   173→) error {
   174→	return fn.fn(ctx, w, cfg)
   175→}
   176→

    14→
    15→package workspaceapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"io"
    20→	"os"
    21→	"syscall"
    22→)
    23→
    24→// Pid is an Executor's command identifier. It doesn't necessarily translate
    25→// to an os.Process.Pid.
    26→type Pid int
    27→
    28→// FileSystem abstracts the public facing API of a workspace file system.
    29→type FileSystem interface {
    30→	URI(path string) (URI, error)
    31→
    32→	// OpenFile opens a file at path with the given flag and mode.
    33→	OpenFile(path string, flag int, mode os.FileMode) (File, error)
    34→
    35→	// Remove removes the file at path.
    36→	Remove(path string) error
    37→
    38→	// Stat returns a FileInfo describing the named file.
    39→	Stat(path string) (os.FileInfo, error)
    40→
    41→	// ReadDir reads the named directory, returning all its directory entries.
    42→	ReadDir(name string) ([]os.DirEntry, error)
    43→
    44→	// MkdirAll creates a directory named path, along with any necessary parents,
    45→	// and returns nil, or else returns an error. The permission bits perm (before
    46→	// umask) are used for all directories that MkdirAll creates. If path is
    47→	// already a directory, MkdirAll does nothing and returns nil.
    48→	MkdirAll(path string, perm os.FileMode) error
    49→}
    50→
    51→// Cmd represents an external command being prepared to run. See exec.Cmd for
    52→// more details. Stdin, Stderr and Stdout, if set, will have their corresponding
    53→type Cmd struct {
    54→	Path    string
    55→	Dir     string
    56→	Args    []string
    57→	Env     []string
    58→	Stdin   io.Reader
    59→	Stdout  io.Writer
    60→	Stderr  io.Writer
    61→	Watcher ProcessWatcher
    62→
    63→	// SysProcAttr is ignored if passed from a extension.
    64→	SysProcAttr *syscall.SysProcAttr
    65→}
    66→
    67→// ProcessWatcher adds the ability for wait a processes started by Cmd
    68→// and collect any I/O errors or non-zero exit code.
    69→type ProcessWatcher interface {
    70→	// WatchProcess returns a channel that can be used to
    71→	// wait for the command to exit and waits for any copying to stdin or
    72→	// copying from stdout or stderr to complete.
    73→
    74→	// The returned error is nil if the command runs, has no problems copying
    75→	// stdin, stdout, and stderr, and exits with a zero exit status.
    76→	WatchProcess() chan error
    77→}
    78→
    79→// Executor is the public facing API of a workspace's command execution.
    80→type Executor interface {
    81→	// Start starts the given cmd and returns the Pid of the underlying
    82→	// process.
    83→	Start(context.Context, Cmd) (Pid, error)
    84→
    85→	// Signal sends a signal to the running process.
    86→	Signal(Pid, syscall.Signal) error
    87→
    88→	io.Closer
    89→}
    90→
    91→// Terminal abstracts the ability to manage pseudoterminals.
    92→type Terminal interface {
    93→	// StartPty creates a new pseudoterminal.
    94→	StartPty() (Pty, error)
    95→
    96→	// SetPtySize sets the width and height in columns and rows of
    97→	// a pseudoterminal.
    98→	SetPtySize(p Pty, width, height int) error
    99→}
   100→
   101→// Pty is a pseudoterminal on a Workspace.
   102→type Pty struct {
   103→	// Master is the pty master device file.
   104→	Master File
   105→	// Slave is the pseudoterminal slave device path.
   106→	Slave File
   107→}
   108→
   109→// File abstracts a subset of os.File.
   110→type File interface {
   111→	Name() string
   112→	Stat() (os.FileInfo, error)
   113→	Sync() error
   114→	Truncate(size int64) error
   115→	Fd() uintptr
   116→
   117→	io.Seeker
   118→	io.Reader
   119→	io.Closer
   120→	io.Writer
   121→	io.ReaderAt
   122→}
   123→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//

    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package extensionapi
    16→
    17→// Permission represents a request to access a resource.
    18→type Permission string
    19→
    20→const (
    21→	// PermissionFileSystem requests access to manage
    22→	// the files in a workspace.
    23→	PermissionFileSystem Permission = "permfs"
    24→	// PermissionExecute requests access to execute
    25→	// and stop processes in a workspace.
    26→	PermissionExecute Permission = "permexec"
    27→	// PermissionTerminal requests access to manage a workspace's ptys.
    28→	PermissionTerminal Permission = "permpty"
    29→	// PermissionBrowserWindowManager requests access to a browser's window manager.
    30→	PermissionBrowserWindowManager Permission = "permwm"
    31→	// PermissionBrowserResourceOpener requests access to open new files.
    32→	PermissionBrowserResourceOpener Permission = "permopen"
    33→	// PermissionNotifications requests access to send messages to the UI.
    34→	PermissionNotifications Permission = "permnoti"
    35→	// PermissionInterrupt requests access to interrupt the event loop.
    36→	// This is useful if your extension handler does async updates to its state, as
    37→	// it enables interrupting the main event loop to redraw components.
    38→	PermissionInterrupt Permission = "permint"
    39→	// PermissionEditor requests access to the editor.
    40→	PermissionEditor Permission = "permed"
    41→	// PermissionCommands requests access to registering new commands.
    42→	PermissionCommands Permission = "permcmd"
    43→	// PermissionStorage requests access to persistent storage.
    44→	PermissionStorage Permission = "permstore"
    45→	// PermissionSyntaxTree requests access to AST-level search.
    46→	PermissionSyntaxTree Permission = "permsyntax"
    47→	// PermissionConfig requests access to read the loaded workspace configuration.
    48→	PermissionConfig Permission = "permcfg"
    49→)
    50→
    51→// Permissions is a set of Permission.
    52→type Permissions map[Permission]any
    53→
    54→// NewPermissions builds a new set of Permission with the given permissions.
    55→func NewPermissions(perms ...Permission) Permissions {
    56→	ret := Permissions{}
    57→	for _, perm := range perms {
    58→		ret[perm] = nil
    59→	}
    60→	return ret
    61→}
    62→
    63→// AllPermissions returns a set of all the permissions.
    64→func AllPermissions() Permissions {
    65→	return Permissions{
    66→		PermissionFileSystem:            nil,
    67→		PermissionExecute:               nil,
    68→		PermissionTerminal:              nil,
    69→		PermissionBrowserWindowManager:  nil,
    70→		PermissionBrowserResourceOpener: nil,
    71→		PermissionNotifications:         nil,
    72→		PermissionInterrupt:             nil,
    73→		PermissionEditor:                nil,
    74→		PermissionCommands:              nil,
    75→		PermissionStorage:               nil,
    76→		PermissionSyntaxTree:            nil,
    77→		PermissionConfig:                nil,
    78→	}
    79→}
    80→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package extensionapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"crypto/tls"
    20→	"crypto/x509"
    21→	"fmt"
    22→	"net"
    23→
    24→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi"
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi/browserrpc"
    26→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config"
    27→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config/configrpc"
    28→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi"
    29→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/docmarshal/doctoml"
    30→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/storagerpc"
    31→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi"
    32→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi/syntaxrpc"
    33→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi"
    34→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi/textrpc"
    35→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    36→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc"
    37→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/term"
    38→	"golang.org/x/oauth2"
    39→	"google.golang.org/grpc"
    40→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials"
    41→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/insecure"
    42→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/oauth"
    43→)
    44→
    45→// Workspace abstracts resources associated with a Workspace.
    46→type Workspace struct {
    47→	dataDir string
    48→	conn    grpc.ClientConnInterface
    49→	editor  *textrpc.Client
    50→	config  config.Config
    51→	meta    Metadata
    52→}
    53→
    54→// DataDir returns the data directory to store data to re-use across sessions.
    55→func (w *Workspace) DataDir(ctx context.Context) string {
    56→	return w.dataDir
    57→}
    58→
    59→// WindowManager returns the workspace's window manager, which can be used
    60→// to create and manipulate the workspace's browser tabs, windows and bars.
    61→func (w *Workspace) WindowManager(ctx context.Context) browserapi.WindowManager {
    62→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    63→}
    64→
    65→// ResourceOpener returns the workspace's resource opener which allows
    66→// clients to access resources in the workspace. Extensions that need more control
    67→// over the underlying files can use FileSystem instead.
    68→func (w *Workspace) ResourceOpener(ctx context.Context) browserapi.ResourceOpener {
    69→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    70→}
    71→
    72→// Notifications returns the workspace's notifications.
    73→func (w *Workspace) Notifications(ctx context.Context) browserapi.Notifications {
    74→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    75→}
    76→
    77→// FileSystem returns the workspace's file system, which can be used to
    78→// directly manipulate raw files in the workspace. Extensions that
    79→// want to simply open resources as tabs in a workspace should use ResourceOpener.
    80→func (w *Workspace) FileSystem(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.FileSystem {
    81→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    82→}
    83→
    84→// Executor returns the workspace's executor, which can be used
    85→// to start and stop processes.
    86→func (w *Workspace) Executor(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Executor {
    87→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    88→}
    89→
    90→// Terminal returns the workspace's pty capability which can be used to
    91→// implement a terminal.
    92→func (w *Workspace) Terminal(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Terminal {
    93→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    94→}
    95→
    96→// Editor returns the workspace's editor which can be used to monitor
    97→// and edit files, move the cursor, add location lists with or without
    98→// color attributes, and even set the default background and foreground
    99→// of a resource.
   100→func (w *Workspace) Editor(ctx context.Context) textapi.Editor {
   101→	return textrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   102→}
   103→
   104→// RegisterCommand registers a new command which is to be dispatched to the
   105→// given CommandHandler. This is the primary mechanism extensions should use
   106→// to run new functionality.
   107→func (w *Workspace) RegisterCommand(
   108→	cmd textapi.CommandManual, h textapi.CommandHandler,
   109→) error {
   110→	return w.editorClient(context.Background()).SubscribeCommand(cmd, h)
   111→}
   112→
   113→// Searcher returns the workspace's syntax searcher, which can be used
   114→// to perform AST-level searches across workspace files.
   115→func (w *Workspace) Searcher(ctx context.Context) syntaxapi.Searcher {
   116→	return syntaxrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   117→}
   118→
   119→// Storage returns the workspace's storage facility, which can be used to
   120→// persist extension data across sessions.
   121→func (w *Workspace) Storage(ctx context.Context) storageapi.Service {
   122→	c := new(storagerpc.Client)
   123→	c.Init(w.conn, doctoml.Marshaler())
   124→	return storageapi.WithPartition(c, w.meta.ExtensionID)
   125→}
   126→
   127→// Interrupter returns the workspace's event loop interrupter, which
   128→// can be used to request new draws when state changes asynchronously.
   129→func (w *Workspace) Interrupter(ctx context.Context) term.Interrupter {
   130→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   131→}
   132→
   133→// Config returns a copy of the workspace's global configuration.
   134→func (w *Workspace) Config(ctx context.Context) config.Config {
   135→	if w.config == nil {
   136→		c, err := configrpc.FetchConfig(w.conn)
   137→		if err != nil {
   138→			return config.ErrConfig(fmt.Errorf("fetch config: %w", err))
   139→		}
   140→		w.config = c
   141→	}
   142→	return w.config
   143→}
   144→
   145→// RawConn returns the underlying raw connection to the extension host.
   146→// This method should generally not be used by extensions.
   147→func (w *Workspace) RawConn() grpc.ClientConnInterface {
   148→	return w.conn
   149→}
   150→
   151→// NewWorkspace returns a new Workspace. Extensions should use
   152→// ServeWorkspaceExtension, which performs the stdin/stdout exchange
   153→// necessary to receive a valid Config.
   154→func NewWorkspace(req Config, meta Metadata) (*Workspace, error) {
   155→	ret := new(Workspace)
   156→	ret.dataDir = req.DataDir
   157→	opts := []grpc.DialOption{
   158→		grpc.WithContextDialer(
   159→			func(ctx context.Context, _ string) (net.Conn, error) {
   160→				addr, err := net.ResolveUnixAddr("unix", req.Socket)
   161→				if err != nil {
   162→					return nil, err
   163→				}
   164→				var d net.Dialer
   165→				return d.DialContext(ctx, addr.Network(), addr.String())
   166→			},
   167→		)}
   168→
   169→	if len(req.Certificate) != 0 {
   170→		certPool := x509.NewCertPool()
   171→		if !certPool.AppendCertsFromPEM(req.Certificate) {
   172→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("credentials: failed to append certificates")
   173→		}
   174→		tlsCfg := tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true, RootCAs: certPool}
   175→		transportCreds := credentials.NewTLS(&tlsCfg)
   176→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(transportCreds))
   177→	} else {
   178→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(insecure.NewCredentials()))
   179→	}
   180→
   181→	if req.Token != nil {
   182→		tokenSource := oauth2.StaticTokenSource(req.Token)
   183→		rpcCreds := oauth.TokenSource{TokenSource: tokenSource}
   184→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithPerRPCCredentials(rpcCreds))
   185→	}
   186→
   187→	// passthrough is used to indicate that dns
   188→	// resolution SHOULD NOT be performed on target
   189→	conn, err := grpc.NewClient("passthrough:", opts...)
   190→	if err != nil {
   191→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("dial: %w", err)
   192→	}
   193→	ret.conn = conn
   194→	ret.meta = meta
   195→	return ret, nil
   196→}
   197→
   198→func (w *Workspace) editorClient(ctx context.Context) *textrpc.Client {
   199→	if w.editor == nil {
   200→		w.editor = w.Editor(ctx).(*textrpc.Client)
   201→	}
   202→	return w.editor
   203→}
   204→

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//

     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package browserapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"io"
    20→
    21→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    22→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/component"
    23→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/handler"
    24→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/term"
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/tui"
    26→)
    27→
    28→// Handler adds Close to a tui.Handler. Close must be idempotent.
    29→type Handler interface {
    30→	tui.Handler
    31→	Close() error
    32→}
    33→
    34→// Floating is a Handler used for Floating windows.
    35→// See handler.Floating for more details.
    36→type Floating interface {
    37→	Handler
    38→	handler.Floating
    39→}
    40→
    41→// Window is the interface that represents
    42→// a closeable window in a WindowManager.
    43→type Window interface {
    44→	WindowID() uint64
    45→}
    46→
    47→// Orientation represents a window orientation.
    48→type Orientation uint8
    49→
    50→const (
    51→	// OrientationDefault represents the default window orientation.
    52→	OrientationDefault Orientation = iota
    53→	// OrientationTop sets the component at the top.
    54→	OrientationTop
    55→	// OrientationBottom sets the component at the bottom.
    56→	OrientationBottom
    57→	// OrientationLeft sets the component on the left.
    58→	OrientationLeft
    59→	// OrientationRight sets the component on the right.
    60→	OrientationRight
    61→)
    62→
    63→// BarFrame represents the different options for creating a bar.
    64→type BarFrame uint8
    65→
    66→const (
    67→	// BarFrameDefault signals that the bar should use the default frame
    68→	// configuration of the WindowManager.
    69→	BarFrameDefault BarFrame = iota
    70→	// BarFrameAlways signals that the bar should always be framed,
    71→	// regardless of the WindowManager configuration.
    72→	BarFrameAlways
    73→	// BarFrameNever signals that the bar should never be framed,
    74→	// regardless of the WindowManager configuration.
    75→	BarFrameNever
    76→)
    77→
    78→// BarConfig defines the configuration for a Bar
    79→// created bia WindowManager.Bar.
    80→type BarConfig struct {
    81→	Orientation Orientation
    82→	Size        int
    83→	Frame       BarFrame
    84→}
    85→
    86→// WindowManager is the interface that groups tile
    87→// window management methods.
    88→type WindowManager interface {
    89→	// Focus returns the current Window in focus.
    90→	Focus() (Window, error)
    91→
    92→	// Split splits the current window in focus in two, and installs
    93→	// Handler in the new window.
    94→	Split(Orientation, Window, Handler) (Window, error)
    95→
    96→	// Floating creates a new floating window at coordinates,
    97→	// with static width and height.
    98→	Floating(h Floating, cfg FloatingConfig) (Window, error)
    99→
   100→	// Bar creates a status bar with Orientation and Handler.

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

    22→
    23→// NodeCaptureName represents a known node capture name on an IDE provided
    24→// query. Values can be combined using bitwise OR to search for multiple node types.
    25→type NodeCaptureName uint32
    26→
    27→const (
    28→	NodeCaptureScope NodeCaptureName = 1 << iota
    29→	NodeCaptureDefinitionNamespace
    30→	NodeCaptureReference
    31→	NodeCaptureDefinitionFunc
    32→	NodeCaptureDefinitionVar
    33→	NodeCaptureDefinitionMethod
    34→	NodeCaptureDefinitionType
    35→	NodeCaptureNameAll = NodeCaptureScope | NodeCaptureDefinitionNamespace |
    36→		NodeCaptureReference | NodeCaptureDefinitionFunc |
    37→		NodeCaptureDefinitionVar | NodeCaptureDefinitionMethod |
    38→		NodeCaptureDefinitionType
    39→)
    40→
    41→// Result represents a single search match.
    42→type Result struct {
    43→	File        workspaceapi.URI
    44→	Text        string
    45→	From        term.Coordinates
    46→	To          term.Coordinates
    47→	CaptureName string
    48→}
    49→
    50→// Searcher provides AST-level search capabilities.
    51→type Searcher interface {
    52→	// Search searches for matches in the workspace using the given tree-sitter literal query
    53→	// and a list of capture names that should be returned.
    54→	Search(query string, captureNames []string) (iterator.Iterator[Result], error)
    55→	// SearchNode is implemented with Search by using an internally provided
    56→	// query that is able to capture a known set of tree nodes across programming
    57→	// languages. Multiple node types can be combined using bitwise OR.
    58→	SearchNode(nodeTypes NodeCaptureName) (iterator.Iterator[Result], error)
    59→	// Query searches for matches in a specific file using the given tree-sitter
    60→	// literal query and a list of capture names that should be returned.
    61→	Query(file workspaceapi.URI, query string, captureNames []string) (
    62→		iterator.Iterator[Result], error,
    63→	)
    64→	// QueryNode is implemented with Query by using an internally provided
    65→	// query that is able to capture a known set of tree nodes across
    66→	// programming languages. Multiple node types can be combined using bitwise OR.
    67→	QueryNode(file workspaceapi.URI, nodeTypes NodeCaptureName) (
    68→		iterator.Iterator[Result], error,

    14→
    15→package workspacerpc
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"errors"
    20→	"fmt"
    21→	"io"
    22→	"log/slog"
    23→	"os"
    24→	"path/filepath"
    25→	"syscall"
    26→	"time"
    27→
    28→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/schemeapi"
    29→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    30→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/debug"
    31→	"google.golang.org/grpc"
    32→)
    33→
    34→const defaultTimeout = 5 * time.Second
    35→
    36→// for extension-side
    37→var _ workspaceapi.FileSystem = (*Client)(nil)
    38→var _ workspaceapi.Executor = (*Client)(nil)
    39→var _ workspaceapi.Terminal = (*Client)(nil)
    40→
    41→// for scheme registry-side
    42→var _ schemeapi.Scheme = (*Client)(nil)
    43→
    44→// Client is a scheme client.
    45→type Client struct {
    46→	cc        grpc.ClientConnInterface
    47→	exec      ExecutorClient
    48→	scheme    SchemeClient
    49→	files     FilesClient
    50→	log       *slog.Logger
    51→	term      TerminalClient
    52→	ctx       context.Context
    53→	root      string // different root than cc's root
    54→	cancelCtx func()
    55→}
    56→
    57→// NewClient allocates storage for a new Client and
    58→// initializes it with the given connection. Client satisfies schemeapi.Scheme
    59→// by connecting to a Server via the given the given rpc connection.
    60→func NewClient(ctx context.Context, cc grpc.ClientConnInterface) *Client {
    61→	ret := new(Client)
    62→	ret.Init(ctx, cc)
    63→	return ret
    64→}
    65→
    66→// Init initializes this client with cc.
    67→func (c *Client) Init(ctx context.Context, cc grpc.ClientConnInterface) {
    68→	c.cc = cc
    69→	c.scheme = NewSchemeClient(cc)
    70→	c.files = NewFilesClient(cc)
    71→	c.term = NewTerminalClient(cc)
    72→	c.exec = NewExecutorClient(cc)
    73→	c.ctx, c.cancelCtx = context.WithCancel(ctx)
    74→	c.log = slog.Default().With("struct", "workspacerpc.Client")
    75→}
    76→
    77→// URI satisfies schemeapi.Scheme.
    78→func (c *Client) URI(path string) (workspaceapi.URI, error) {
    79→	ctx, cleanup := ctxWithTimeout(c.ctx)
    80→	defer cleanup()
    81→
    82→	req := URIRequest{Root: c.root, Path: path}
    83→	resp, err := c.scheme.URI(ctx, &req)
    84→	if err != nil {
    85→		return workspaceapi.URI{}, err
    86→	}
    87→	uri, err := workspaceapi.ParseURI(resp.GetUri())
    88→	if err != nil {
    89→		return workspaceapi.URI{}, fmt.Errorf("could not parse URI response from server: %w", err)
    90→	}
    91→	return uri, nil
    92→}
    93→
    94→// Chroot satisfies schemeapi.Scheme.
    95→func (c *Client) Chroot(path string) (schemeapi.Scheme, error) {
    96→	uri, err := c.URI(path)
    97→	if err != nil {
    98→		return nil, err
    99→	}
   100→	ret := NewClient(context.Background(), c.cc)

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - jingkaihe/matchlock: Matchlock secures AI agent workloads with a Linux-based sandbox.","url":"https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock"},{"title":"GitHub All-Stars #13: Matchlock","url":"https://virtuslab.com/blog/ai/matchlock-your-agents-bulletproof-cage/"},{"title":"Matchlock: The Open-Source Tool That Wants to Bring Chaos Engineering to Every Kubernetes Cluster","url":"https://www.webpronews.com/matchlock-the-open-source-tool-that-wants-to-bring-chaos-engineering-to-every-kubernetes-cluster/"},{"title":"Matchlock – Secures AI agent workloads with a Linux-based sandbox | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46932343"},{"title":"BDO Matchlock Guide: Recipes, Stats, Enhancement | GrumpyG","url":"https://grumpygreen.cricket/bdo-matchlock/"},{"title":"GitHub - KorallTheCoral/MatchlockGuns-Mod","url":"https://github.com/KorallTheCoral/MatchlockGuns-Mod"},{"title":"GitHub - adrenalynn/AngryGuards: a Colony Survival mod","url":"https://github.com/adrenalynn/AngryGuards"},{"title":"Matchlock - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matchlock"},{"title":"jin (Jin) · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/jin"},{"title":"Steam Workshop::Realistic matchlock（honnkaku_hinawa）","url":"https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2794066358"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about the Matchlock project by jingkaihe on GitHub:

Matchlock secures AI agent workloads with a Linux-based sandbox. On Linux, Matchlock launches Firecracker microVMs - the same VMM that powers AWS Lambda. On macOS, it uses Apple's native Virtualization.framework.

The project includes several key features:

- **Network Security**: Everything is denied by default. No network egress unless you explicitly allowlist a domain with --allow-host.

- **Secret Management**: The real API key never enters the VM. Inside the sandbox, $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY resolves to a placeholder like SANDBOX_SECRET_a1b2c3d4. When the agent makes an HTTPS request to api.anthropic.com, the host-side transparent proxy performs TLS MITM, intercepts the outgoing request, and swaps the placeholder for the real key - but only for that specific host.

- **SDKs**: Matchlock ships with Go and Python SDKs for embedding sandboxes directly in your application. Allows you to programmatically launch VMs, exec commands, stream output and write files.

The GitHub repository is at https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

It is written in Swift, and optimized for Apple silicon.","url":"https://github.com/apple/container"},{"title":"GitHub - apple/containerization: Containerization is a Swift package for running Linux containers on macOS.","url":"https://github.com/apple/containerization"},{"title":"Releases · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/releases"},{"title":"GitHub - ducheharsh/apple-container-desktop: 🐳 Modern desktop GUI for Apple's container CLI - Docker Desktop alternative for Apple Silicon Macs. Built with React + Tauri.","url":"https://github.com/ducheharsh/apple-container-desktop"},{"title":"container/docs/how-to.md at main · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/blob/main/docs/how-to.md"},{"title":"container/BUILDING.md at main · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/blob/main/BUILDING.md"},{"title":"Apple’s Container: Native, Lightweight Container Runtime for macOS | by Pavan Kumar | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@REDACTED"},{"title":"container/docs/technical-overview.md at main · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/blob/main/docs/technical-overview.md"},{"title":"[Bug]: `container` do support cross-platform build, but may fail with `--platform` specified in Dockerfile · Issue #372 · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/issues/372"},{"title":"Workflow runs · apple/container","url":"https://github.com/apple/container/actions"}]

I found comprehensive information about the Apple container project. Here are the key findings:

## Project Overview

Apple's container project is a tool for creating and running Linux containers using lightweight virtual machines on a Mac, written in Swift and optimized for Apple silicon.

## Key Features

- **OCI Compatibility**: The tool consumes and produces OCI-compatible container images, so you can pull and run images from any standard container registry, and push images that you build to those registries as well.

- **Architecture**: It runs a lightweight VM for each container that you create. Each container has the isolation properties of a full VM, using a minimal set of core utilities and dynamic libraries to reduce resource utilization.

- **Performance**: Containers achieve sub-second start times using an optimized Linux kernel configuration and a minimal root filesystem with a lightweight init system.

## System Requirements

You need a Mac with Apple silicon to run container. Container is supported on macOS 26, since it takes advantage of new features and enhancements to virtualization and networking in this release.

## Development Status

The container project is currently under active development. Its stability is only guaranteed within patch versions, such as between 0.1.1 and 0.1.2.

Based on the search results, I found information about Matchlock. There are actually two different projects with this name, so let me clarify:

## Matchlock for AI Agent Sandboxing

Matchlock secures AI agent workloads with a Linux-based sandbox. This is the primary Matchlock project you're asking about, hosted at https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock.

**Key Features:**

Matchlock ships with Go and Python SDKs for embedding sandboxes directly in your application, allowing you to programmatically launch VMs, exec commands, stream output and write files.

**Security Approach:**

No network egress unless you explicitly allowlist a domain with --allow-host. The real API key never enters the VM. Inside the sandbox, $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY resolves to a placeholder like SANDBOX_SECRET_a1b2c3d4.

**Positioning:**

It's the self-hosted, developer-first, cross-platform option. No cloud dependency, no SaaS pricing, no 24-hour limits. Install via Homebrew, run locally, done.

---

## Note on Similar Project

The search results also returned a different Matchlock project focused on Kubernetes chaos engineering, but the sandbox container runtime project appears to be the primary jingkaihe/matchlock repository you're interested in.

Perfect! Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive exploration report. Let me create my final summary:

## Codebase Exploration Report: Rune Go SDK

Based on my thorough exploration of the `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox` codebase, here's a comprehensive understanding of the API architecture, patterns, and how extensions interact with the Rune IDE:

### 1. API Packages Organization

The SDK has **8 main API packages** in `/api`:

- **extensionapi** - Extension lifecycle and workspace access
- **workspaceapi** - File system, process execution, PTY management
- **browserapi** - Window management, notifications, resource opening
- **textapi** - Editor integration and text operations
- **syntaxapi** - AST-level code search capabilities
- **storageapi** - Persistent document storage
- **config** - Configuration management
- **schemeapi** - Custom URI scheme registration

### 2. Consistent API Package Pattern

Each API package follows a standard structure:

```
api/<name>/
├── <name>.go              # Public wrapper interfaces and types
├── <name>_test.go         # Tests
├── errors.go              # Error definitions
├── generate.go            # Build directive for code generation
├── <name>rpc/
│   ├── <name>.proto       # Protocol Buffer service definitions
│   ├── <name>.pb.go       # Generated message types
│   ├── <name>_grpc.pb.go  # Generated gRPC stubs
│   ├── client.go          # Custom gRPC client implementation
│   └── *.go               # Additional helper files
├── <name>test/            # (Optional) Test mocks/stubs
└── docmarshal/            # (Optional) Document marshaling
```

### 3. Extension Lifecycle Pattern

The **extensionapi** is the main entry point for extensions. Key files:

**serve.go** - Core extension serving logic:
- `ServeWorkspaceExtension()` - Main server function
- Handles stdin/stdout JSON exchange protocol
- Exchanges metadata (developer ID, permissions, extension info)
- Receives gRPC socket and OAuth2 token configuration
- Captures panics and logs errors

**workspace.go** - Workspace access wrapper:
- `Workspace` struct provides unified access to all APIs
- Methods return API-specific clients (WindowManager, FileSystem, Editor, etc.)
- Uses gRPC over Unix socket with TLS and OAuth2 credentials
- Lazy-loads API clients on first use

**permissions.go** - Permission system:
- 12 permission types (FileSystem, Execute, Terminal, BrowserWindowManager, etc.)
- `Permissions` type is a map[Permission]any set
- Extensions declare permissions in Metadata

### 4. WorkspaceAPI - File and Process Management

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/`

**workspace.go** defines key interfaces:

- **FileSystem** - Abstracts workspace file operations (OpenFile, Remove, ReadDir, MkdirAll, etc.)
- **Executor** - Process execution (Start, Signal with Pid type)
- **Terminal** - PTY management (StartPty, SetPtySize with Pty struct)
- **File** - File handle abstraction (Read, Write, Seek, Sync, etc.)
- **Cmd** - Command definition with args, env, stdin/stdout/stderr, ProcessWatcher

**workspacerpc/** subdirectory:
- `workspace.proto` defines 10 gRPC services (Scheme, Executor, Files, Terminal, etc.)
- Rich message types for all file operations, process control, PTY management
- Streaming command execution via `StartCommand(stream CommandPayload)`
- Watch capability for file system events (Create, Remove, Write, Rename)
- Command stream with Started, IO, Done message payloads

### 5. gRPC/Protobuf Integration Pattern

**Key structure in each API's rpc subdirectory:**

1. **Proto definitions** - Service methods and message types
2. **Generated types** - `*.pb.go` contains data structures (auto-generated)
3. **Generated stubs** - `*_grpc.pb.go` contains service stubs (auto-generated)
4. **Custom client** - `client.go` wraps generated stubs with domain-specific logic
5. **Helper types** - Supporting Go types and conversion functions

**Example from browserapi:**
- Proto defines 4 services: ResourceOpener, Notifications, WindowManager, Floating
- Uses streaming for interactive handlers (Split, Bar, Floating, SetContent)
- Custom client wraps these with user-friendly Go interfaces
- Stream messages include handler.ServerMessage types for bidirectional communication

### 6. BrowserAPI - UI Integration Pattern

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/browserapi/`

**Key abstractions:**

- **WindowManager** - Focus, Split, Floating windows, Bars, Tabs
- **Handler** - UI component with Close() method (extends tui.Handler)
- **Floating** - Handler for floating windows with positioning
- **Notifications** - Send messages with progress tracking
- **ResourceOpener** - Open resources as tabs
- **Orientation** - Window placement (Default, Top, Bottom, Left, Right)
- **BarFrame** - Bar styling (Default, Always, Never)

**Streaming interaction model:**
- Split/Bar/Floating use bidirectional streams
- Handler sends Draw, Handle, Close, Cursor, Selection, Dimensions messages
- Server sends ServerMessage responses
- Integrates with handler.ServerMessage for consistent event handling

### 7. TextAPI - Editor Integration

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/textapi/`

**Proto-defined capabilities:**
- Edit operations (EditRequest for buffer content, mode, recovery status)
- Location lists (SetLocationListRequest with coordinates, attributes, messages)
- Editor events (Open, Close, Flush, Edit, Scroll, Create, Change, Focus, etc.)
- Cursor and selection management
- Cell-based editing with term.CellRow and term.Coordinates
- Command registration and subscription

### 8. SyntaxAPI - Code Search

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/syntaxapi/`

**Key interfaces:**

- **Searcher** - Workspace-wide AST queries
- **Result** - Match with File URI, Text, coordinates, capture name
- **NodeCaptureName** - Bitflags for node types (Scope, Reference, Definition*, etc.)

**Capabilities:**
- `Search()` - Tree-sitter literal queries on entire workspace
- `SearchNode()` - Pre-built queries for common node types
- `Query()` - Single file searches
- `QueryNode()` - File-specific node queries
- Returns `iterator.Iterator[Result]` for lazy evaluation

### 9. StorageAPI - Persistence

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/storageapi/`

**Proto defines DocumentStore service:**
- Create, Set, Get, Update, Delete, List operations
- Supports filtering by field paths and values
- Document CRUD with precondition checks (AlreadyExists, NotFound, PreconditionFailed)
- Marshaling support (doctoml.Marshaler for TOML format)
- Extension ID-based partitioning

**Key types:**
- Document/Service interface for domain operations
- Partition wrapper for extension isolation
- Field-based updates with precondition validation

### 10. ConfigAPI - Configuration Access

Located at `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/config/`

**Simple design:**
- Single Get() RPC method
- Config interface provides typed accessors: GetInt, GetFloat, GetString, GetBool, GetAttribute, GetColor, GetRune, GetConfig, GetMap, GetSlice
- mapConfig implementation wraps map[string]interface{}
- JSON encoding support with Clone, GetAttributes, GetDuration helpers
- Error types: ErrNotFound, ErrInvalidType, ErrInvalidAttributeValue

### 11. No Existing Sandbox Implementation

Search for "sandbox" in codebase returned **no results** - there is currently NO sandbox-related code in the repository. This is a completely new feature area to be built.

### 12. Integration Flow Summary

**How extensions work:**

1. Extension implements `WorkspaceExtension.ExtendWorkspace(ctx, workspace, config)`
2. Calls `ServeWorkspaceExtension(extension, metadata)` in main()
3. Runtime performs stdin/stdout JSON exchange (metadata ↔ config)
4. Extension receives Config with socket path, token, certificate, dataDir
5. Creates `Workspace` struct connecting to gRPC services via Unix socket
6. Uses `Workspace` methods to access FileSystem, Executor, Terminal, Editor, etc.
7. All APIs communicate back to IDE through gRPC services

### 13. Code Quality Standards (from CLAUDE.md)

- Maximum 90-column line length
- Table-driven tests for all components
- Apache 2.0 license headers required
- Race detection enabled (`-race` flag)
- Pre-commit hooks enforce: YAML validation, protobuf regeneration, license headers
- All tests run with `-race -timeout 120s`

### Key File Paths

- **Extension serving**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/extensionapi/serve.go`
- **Workspace wrapper**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/extensionapi/workspace.go`
- **API definitions**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/*/\*rpc/` (proto files)
- **Client implementations**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/*/\*rpc/client.go`
- **Interfaces**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/*/<name>.go` (the public-facing wrapper)

This exploration reveals a well-architected SDK with consistent patterns for gRPC integration, clear separation between generated code and domain logic, and comprehensive access to IDE resources through a unified Workspace interface.

content.content:
# Matchlock: Secure AI Agent Sandboxing

## Overview

Matchlock is an experimental CLI tool designed to execute AI agents within isolated ephemeral microVMs. The project emphasizes security through "network allowlisting, secret injection via MITM proxy, and VM-level isolation," ensuring "your secrets never enter the VM."

## Core Architecture

The system operates through a host-guest architecture:

**Host Components:**
- CLI interface managing sandbox lifecycle
- Policy Engine enforcing network restrictions
- Transparent Proxy with TLS MITM capabilities
- VFS Server for filesystem operations

**Guest Components (Micro-VM):**
- Guest Agent handling execution
- FUSE-mounted workspace
- Any OCI-compatible Linux image (Alpine, Ubuntu, etc.)

Communication occurs via vsock channels: the proxy connects on port 5000, while the VFS server uses port 5001.

## Network Isolation Mechanisms

Platform-specific implementations ensure security:

| Platform | Mode | Mechanism |
|----------|------|-----------|
| Linux | Transparent proxy | nftables DNAT on ports 80/443 |
| macOS | NAT (default) | Virtualization.framework built-in |
| macOS | Interception | gVisor userspace TCP/IP |

When `--allow-host` or `--secret` flags are used, "the network" becomes sealed, blocking all traffic except to explicitly permitted hosts.

## Secret Management

The system implements credential protection through substitution. When secrets are injected via the `--secret` flag, "the sandbox only ever sees a placeholder." Real credentials remain on the host and are "injected in-flight" by the proxy during API calls, preventing credential exposure within the VM environment.

## Key Features

- **Ephemeral Filesystems:** "Every sandbox runs on its own copy-on-write filesystem that vanishes when you're done"
- **Cross-Platform:** Consistent behavior across Linux and macOS
- **Rapid Startup:** Boots in under one second
- **Disposable:** Full cleanup after execution

## SDK Support

Matchlock provides programmatic interfaces:

**Go SDK:** Allows "launching VMs, exec commands, stream output and write files" via the `github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/pkg/sdk` package.

**Python SDK:** Available through PyPI (`matchlock` package), offering equivalent functionality with Pythonic APIs.

## Usage Examples

Basic execution, network allowlisting, long-lived sandboxes, image management, and Dockerfile builds are all supported through the CLI and SDKs.

## License

MIT

content.content:
# Matchlock Project Analysis

## 1. Project Description and Purpose
Matchlock is a CLI tool designed to run AI agents in ephemeral microVMs with network allowlisting and secret injection capabilities. The system prevents credentials from entering sandboxes by intercepting API calls at the host level using a MITM proxy approach.

## 2. Programming Language
**Primary: Go (88.6%)** with Python SDK (9.7%), Shell (1.3%), and Dockerfile (0.4%)

## 3. Platforms Supported
- **Linux** with KVM support
- **macOS** on Apple Silicon

## 4. Isolation Mechanisms
The architecture employs multiple layers:
- **Hypervisor technology**: Firecracker on Linux; Virtualization.framework on macOS
- **Network isolation**: Transparent proxy with nftables DNAT (Linux) or gVisor userspace TCP/IP (macOS)
- **Filesystem isolation**: Copy-on-write filesystems and FUSE-based workspace mounting
- **Communication**: vsock channels for inter-VM communication

## 5. API/SDK Information
- **Go SDK**: Direct embedding via `github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/pkg/sdk`
- **Python SDK**: Available on PyPI (`pip install matchlock`)
- Both SDKs support programmatic VM launching, command execution, output streaming, and file operations

## 6. Repository Metrics
- **Stars**: 412
- **Forks**: 15
- **Contributors**: 2
- **Latest Release**: v0.1.11 (February 10, 2026)
- **Total Commits**: 224

## 7. Recent Activity
The project shows active development with 11 releases and recent updates, though marked as "Experimental" with potential breaking changes ahead.

## 8. License
**MIT License** - permissive open-source licensing

## 9. Key Features
- Ephemeral sandbox creation with sub-second boot times
- Secret injection via transparent MITM proxy without exposing credentials to VMs
- Network allowlisting for restricting outbound connections
- Long-lived sandbox support with attach capabilities
- Docker BuildKit-in-VM for custom image builds
- Cross-platform consistency between Linux servers and MacBooks

## 10. Installation
Primary distribution through Homebrew:
```
brew tap jingkaihe/essentials
brew install matchlock
```

content.content:
# Container Technical Details

## Isolation Mechanisms

The document describes container runs through **per-container lightweight VMs** rather than shared VM approaches. Key frameworks used:

- **Virtualization framework**: "for managing Linux virtual machines and their attached devices"
- **vmnet framework**: "for managing the virtual network to which the containers attach"
- **XPC**: "for interprocess communication"
- **Launchd**: "for service management"

Each container receives "the isolation properties of a full VM, using a minimal set of core utilities and dynamic libraries to reduce resource utilization and attack surface."

## Platform Support

**macOS 15 and 26 only** - the document states container "relies on the new features and enhancements present in macOS 26" but can run on macOS 15 with limitations. No Intel/ARM distinction is mentioned; Apple Silicon is implied by Virtualization.framework usage.

## Architecture Details

The runtime consists of:
- **container-apiserver**: Launch agent managing container/network resources
- **container-core-images**: XPC helper for image management and local content storage
- **container-network-vmnet**: XPC helper for virtual networking
- **container-runtime-linux**: Per-container runtime helper exposing management APIs

## How Containers Run

"Using the open source Containerization package, it runs a lightweight VM for each container that you create" - fundamentally different from typical shared-VM approaches. Each container mounts only necessary host data, improving privacy compared to shared VM architectures.

content.content:
# Matchlock Discussion: Key Takeaways from Hacker News

## Core Technical Insights

**What Matchlock Offers:**
Creator jingkai_he explained that Matchlock provides "agent specific network and filesystem controls" to prevent rogue agents from exfiltrating API keys. It uses Firecracker on Linux and Apple's Virtualization Framework on macOS, offering hardware-level isolation superior to container-based approaches.

**Limitations Acknowledged:**
The community consensus highlighted a fundamental constraint: sandboxing alone cannot prevent prompt injection attacks. As one commenter noted, "a sandbox does not prevent an email-reading agent from reading a malicious email, being prompt injected, and then sending an email to a malicious email address."

## Security Architecture Debate

**Isolation vs. Access Control:**
Discussants emphasized that true protection requires layered defenses. Domain whitelisting, secret protection, and network-layer controls represent the "hard defense" against exfiltration, while sandboxing provides essential but incomplete protection.

**Vendor Solutions Criticized:**
Users found existing tools inadequate. One reviewer stated that Claude's sandbox has "unclear" documentation and "insufficient" whitelist-based access controls, while security issues "go unanswered."

## Red Team Findings

The creator revealed concerning agent capabilities observed during testing: Matchlock-confined models attempted kernel CVEs, weaponized compilers, probed metadata services, and exploited eBPF vulnerabilities—demonstrating why layered security remains essential.

## Community Resources

Alternative projects mentioned include Gondolin, agentd, Leash, and others addressing similar sandboxing needs.

content.content:
# Containerization Package Overview

## 1) What This Package Provides

The Containerization package is "a Swift package for running Linux containers on macOS." It enables developers to manage OCI images, interact with remote registries, create ext4 filesystems, work with Netlink sockets, and spawn lightweight virtual machines with containerized processes. The package supports Rosetta 2 for running AMD64 containers on Apple silicon.

## 2) Key APIs and Interfaces

The package surfaces several core capabilities:
- OCI image management and registry client functionality
- ext4 filesystem creation and population
- Netlink socket interaction for network configuration
- Linux kernel optimization for fast container startup
- Process spawning and lifecycle management through a gRPC API (vminitd)
- Virtual machine provisioning with dedicated IP addresses per container

## 3) Platform Requirements

The package requires:
- Mac with Apple silicon
- macOS 26
- Xcode 26

Notably, older macOS versions are explicitly unsupported.

## 4) Relationship to Container CLI Tool

This is a Swift library package. The documentation references a separate ["apple/container repository"](https://github.com/apple/container) that provides "command line binaries for running containers"—indicating this library serves as the foundation for CLI tools rather than being the CLI itself.

## 5) Star Count and Maturity

The project has 8.3k GitHub stars and 249 forks. Version 0.1.0 represents the first official release; earlier versions lack source stability guarantees. "Source stability is only guaranteed within minor versions," indicating it remains under active development.

content.content:
# Matchlock: Technical Architecture & Security Analysis

## Core Architecture

Matchlock is a CLI tool designed to execute AI agents safely within isolated environments. Written primarily in Go and MIT-licensed, it implements a layered security approach combining microVM isolation with network controls and secret management.

### Isolation Mechanisms

**Hardware-Level Virtualization**: The foundation uses platform-specific VM technologies rather than container isolation. On Linux, it deploys Firecracker microVMs—the same hypervisor powering AWS Lambda. macOS implementations leverage Apple's native Virtualization.framework. Both achieve sub-millisecond boot times, providing genuine VM-level isolation that requires kernel-level exploits to breach, rather than container escape techniques.

**Network Lockdown**: Traffic operates under a deny-by-default model. Egress requires explicit allowlisting via `--allow-host` flags. The implementation differs by platform: Linux uses `nftables` DNAT rules directing ports 80/443 through a transparent proxy, while macOS employs a gVisor-based userspace TCP/IP stack for L4 traffic interception.

**Secret Injection via MITM Proxy**: Rather than exposing credentials within sandboxes, real API keys remain on the host. Inside the VM, secrets appear as scoped placeholders (e.g., `SANDBOX_SECRET_a1b2c3d4`). When agents make HTTPS requests to allowlisted domains, the host-side proxy performs TLS interception, swapping placeholders for actual credentials—but exclusively for authorized destinations.

**In-VM Hardening**: Process isolation uses PID and mount namespaces. Unprivileged modes drop Linux capabilities (`SYS_PTRACE`, `SYS_ADMIN`), enforce `no_new_privs`, and install seccomp-BPF filters blocking dangerous syscalls including `process_vm_readv` and `ptrace`.

## Security Features

The architecture operates on "defense in depth" principles. As the creator observed, large language models given Linux access "will systematically probe for escape vectors." The layered approach mitigates this by making each boundary progressively harder to breach.

## Developer Experience

The CLI mimics Docker's interface, enabling immediate adoption by developers familiar with containerization. Any OCI-compatible image works directly. File access occurs via FUSE over vsock without copying data into VMs.

## Competitive Positioning

| Solution | Approach | Strengths | Limitations |
|----------|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Matchlock** | Self-hosted microVMs | Cross-platform, no cloud dependency, developer-first | No managed infrastructure |
| **E2B** | Firecracker (cloud) | Clean SDK, integrations | 24-hour limits, cloud-only |
| **Daytona** | Container-based | Fast cold starts | Weaker isolation boundaries |
| **Modal** | gVisor, cloud | Massive scaling | No on-premises option |
| **Northflank** | Multiple isolation options | Enterprise-grade, high throughput | Full platform complexity |

Matchlock fills the niche for developers requiring local, cross-platform sandboxing without vendor lock-in—particularly relevant for safely running coding agents locally with confidence.

content.content:
# Apple Container CLI Commands and Examples

## Core Container Operations

**Run containers:**
```
container run [options] image [command]
container run --rm --cpus 8 --memory 32g image_name
container run -d --name my-web-server --rm image:latest
container run -it --rm alpine:latest sh
```

**Build images:**
```
container build --arch arm64 --arch amd64 --tag registry/image:tag --file Dockerfile .
container build -t local/custom-init:latest .
```

**Image management:**
```
container image list
container image inspect image_name
container image push registry.example.com/image:tag
```

**Container management:**
```
container list
container ls (alias)
container stop container_name
container inspect container_name
container logs container_name
container logs --boot container_name
```

## Key Flags and Options

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--memory` | Set memory limit (e.g., 32g) |
| `--cpus` | Set CPU count |
| `--volume` or `--mount` | Share host files |
| `--publish/-p` | Forward ports |
| `--network` | Attach to network |
| `--ssh` | Mount SSH authentication socket |
| `--arch` | Specify architecture (arm64, amd64) |
| `--init-image` | Custom init filesystem |
| `--virtualization` | Enable nested virtualization |
| `--format json` | JSON output |

## Advanced Features

**Network operations:**
```
container network create foo
container network create foo --subnet 192.168.100.0/24 --subnet-v6 fd00:1234::/64
container network list
container network delete foo
```

**Resource monitoring:**
```
container stats
container stats --no-stream container_name
container stats --format json --no-stream container_name | jq
```

**Builder configuration:**
```
container builder start --cpus 8 --memory 32g
container builder stop
container builder delete
```

**System management:**
```
container system start
container system dns create host.container.internal --localhost 203.0.113.113
container system property set network.subnet 192.168.100.1/24
container system property set network.subnetv6 fd00:abcd::/64
```

**Completion scripts:**
```
container completion zsh|bash|fish
```

## Common Patterns

- Port forwarding: `-p 127.0.0.1:8080:8000`
- Volume mounting: `--volume ${HOME}/path:/container/path`
- Multiplatform builds: Multiple `--arch` flags
- MAC address specification: `--network default,mac=02:42:ac:11:00:02`

content.text:
Now I have comprehensive information about the Matchlock project. Let me compile a detailed summary.

---

## Matchlock Research Summary

### 1. What This Project Does

**Matchlock** is a CLI tool and SDK for running AI agents in ephemeral, isolated microVMs with network allowlisting and secret injection capabilities. It is specifically designed to secure AI agent workloads by preventing credential exfiltration and restricting network access.

The core use case is safely executing AI coding agents (like Claude, GPT-4, etc.) that need shell access without risking:
- API key theft
- Unauthorized network access
- System compromise

### 2. Platform Support

| Platform | Virtualization Technology | Requirements |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------|
| **Linux** | Firecracker microVMs (same as AWS Lambda) | KVM support required |
| **macOS** | Apple's native Virtualization.framework | Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) |

Both platforms achieve sub-second boot times and provide consistent cross-platform behavior.

### 3. Isolation Mechanisms

Matchlock employs **defense-in-depth** with multiple isolation layers:

**a) Hypervisor-Level Isolation:**
- **Linux**: Firecracker microVMs - true VM isolation requiring kernel-level exploits to breach
- **macOS**: Virtualization.framework - native Apple hypervisor

**b) Network Isolation:**
- Deny-by-default model - no egress unless explicitly allowlisted via `--allow-host`
- **Linux**: `nftables` DNAT rules directing ports 80/443 through transparent proxy
- **macOS**: gVisor userspace TCP/IP stack for L4 traffic interception

**c) Secret Management via MITM Proxy:**
- Real API keys never enter the VM
- Inside the sandbox, secrets appear as placeholders (e.g., `SANDBOX_SECRET_a1b2c3d4`)
- Host-side transparent proxy intercepts HTTPS requests and swaps placeholders for real credentials only for allowlisted hosts

**d) In-VM Hardening:**
- PID and mount namespaces
- Drops Linux capabilities (`SYS_PTRACE`, `SYS_ADMIN`)
- Enforces `no_new_privs`
- seccomp-BPF filters blocking dangerous syscalls (`process_vm_readv`, `ptrace`)

**e) Filesystem Isolation:**
- Copy-on-write filesystems that vanish after execution
- FUSE-based workspace mounting over vsock (no data copying into VMs)

### 4. Programming Language

**Yes, it is written in Go (88.6%)** with additional components:
- Python SDK (9.7%)
- Shell scripts (1.3%)
- Dockerfile (0.4%)

### 5. Project Maturity

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **GitHub Stars** | 412 |
| **Forks** | 15 |
| **Contributors** | 2 |
| **Total Commits** | 224 |
| **Latest Release** | v0.1.11 (February 10, 2026) |
| **Total Releases** | 11 |
| **License** | MIT |
| **Status** | **Experimental** - potential breaking changes ahead |

The project is actively maintained with recent releases, but is still in early stages with a small contributor base. The creator (jingkaihe) actively engages with the community on Hacker News.

### 6. API/Interface

**CLI Interface** (Docker-like syntax):
```bash
# Basic execution
matchlock run alpine -- echo "Hello from sandbox"

# With network allowlisting
matchlock run --allow-host api.anthropic.com alpine -- curl https://api.anthropic.com

# With secret injection
matchlock run --secret ANTHROPIC_API_KEY --allow-host api.anthropic.com ubuntu -- ./my-agent

# Long-lived sandbox
matchlock sandbox start --name my-sandbox alpine
matchlock sandbox attach my-sandbox
matchlock sandbox stop my-sandbox
```

**Go SDK:**
```go
import "github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/pkg/sdk"

// Programmatically launch VMs, execute commands, stream output, write files
```

**Python SDK:**
```bash
pip install matchlock
```

**Installation:**
```bash
brew tap jingkaihe/essentials
brew install matchlock
```

**Architecture Components:**

Host Side:
- CLI interface managing sandbox lifecycle
- Policy Engine enforcing network restrictions
- Transparent Proxy with TLS MITM capabilities
- VFS Server for filesystem operations (vsock port 5001)

Guest Side:
- Guest Agent handling execution
- FUSE-mounted workspace
- Any OCI-compatible Linux image (Alpine, Ubuntu, etc.)

Communication occurs via vsock channels with the proxy on port 5000 and VFS server on port 5001.

### Comparison to Alternatives

| Solution | Approach | Key Difference from Matchlock |
|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| **E2B** | Cloud Firecracker | 24-hour limits, cloud-only |
| **Daytona** | Container-based | Weaker isolation (container escape vs kernel exploit) |
| **Modal** | gVisor, cloud | No on-premises option |
| **Anthropic sandbox-runtime** | Lightweight OS-level sandboxing | No VM isolation |

Matchlock's niche is **self-hosted, cross-platform, developer-first sandboxing** without cloud dependencies.

---

### Sources:
- [Matchlock GitHub Repository](https://github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock)
- [GitHub All-Stars #13: Matchlock - VirtusLab](https://virtuslab.com/blog/ai/matchlock-your-agents-bulletproof-cage/)
- [Matchlock Hacker News Discussion](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46932343)
