    15→package extensionapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"crypto/tls"
    20→	"crypto/x509"
    21→	"fmt"
    22→	"net"
    23→
    24→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi"
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi/browserrpc"
    26→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config"
    27→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config/configrpc"
    28→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi"
    29→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/docmarshal/doctoml"
    30→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/storagerpc"
    31→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi"
    32→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi/syntaxrpc"
    33→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi"
    34→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi/textrpc"
    35→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    36→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc"
    37→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/term"
    38→	"golang.org/x/oauth2"
    39→	"google.golang.org/grpc"
    40→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials"
    41→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/insecure"
    42→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/oauth"
    43→)
    44→
    45→// Workspace abstracts resources associated with a Workspace.
    46→type Workspace struct {
    47→	dataDir string
    48→	conn    grpc.ClientConnInterface
    49→	editor  *textrpc.Client
    50→	config  config.Config
    51→	meta    Metadata
    52→}
    53→
    54→// DataDir returns the data directory to store data to re-use across sessions.
    55→func (w *Workspace) DataDir(ctx context.Context) string {
    56→	return w.dataDir
    57→}
    58→
    59→// WindowManager returns the workspace's window manager, which can be used
    60→// to create and manipulate the workspace's browser tabs, windows and bars.
    61→func (w *Workspace) WindowManager(ctx context.Context) browserapi.WindowManager {
    62→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    63→}
    64→
    65→// ResourceOpener returns the workspace's resource opener which allows
    66→// clients to access resources in the workspace. Extensions that need more control
    67→// over the underlying files can use FileSystem instead.
    68→func (w *Workspace) ResourceOpener(ctx context.Context) browserapi.ResourceOpener {
    69→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    70→}
    71→
    72→// Notifications returns the workspace's notifications.
    73→func (w *Workspace) Notifications(ctx context.Context) browserapi.Notifications {
    74→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    75→}
    76→
    77→// FileSystem returns the workspace's file system, which can be used to
    78→// directly manipulate raw files in the workspace. Extensions that
    79→// want to simply open resources as tabs in a workspace should use ResourceOpener.
    80→func (w *Workspace) FileSystem(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.FileSystem {
    81→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    82→}
    83→
    84→// Executor returns the workspace's executor, which can be used
    85→// to start and stop processes.
    86→func (w *Workspace) Executor(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Executor {
    87→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    88→}
    89→
    90→// Terminal returns the workspace's pty capability which can be used to
    91→// implement a terminal.
    92→func (w *Workspace) Terminal(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Terminal {
    93→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    94→}
    95→
    96→// Editor returns the workspace's editor which can be used to monitor
    97→// and edit files, move the cursor, add location lists with or without
    98→// color attributes, and even set the default background and foreground
    99→// of a resource.
   100→func (w *Workspace) Editor(ctx context.Context) textapi.Editor {
   101→	return textrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   102→}
   103→
   104→// RegisterCommand registers a new command which is to be dispatched to the
   105→// given CommandHandler. This is the primary mechanism extensions should use
   106→// to run new functionality.
   107→func (w *Workspace) RegisterCommand(
   108→	cmd textapi.CommandManual, h textapi.CommandHandler,
   109→) error {
   110→	return w.editorClient(context.Background()).SubscribeCommand(cmd, h)
   111→}
   112→
   113→// Searcher returns the workspace's syntax searcher, which can be used
   114→// to perform AST-level searches across workspace files.
   115→func (w *Workspace) Searcher(ctx context.Context) syntaxapi.Searcher {
   116→	return syntaxrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   117→}
   118→
   119→// Storage returns the workspace's storage facility, which can be used to
   120→// persist extension data across sessions.
   121→func (w *Workspace) Storage(ctx context.Context) storageapi.Service {
   122→	c := new(storagerpc.Client)
   123→	c.Init(w.conn, doctoml.Marshaler())
   124→	return storageapi.WithPartition(c, w.meta.ExtensionID)
   125→}
   126→
   127→// Interrupter returns the workspace's event loop interrupter, which
   128→// can be used to request new draws when state changes asynchronously.
   129→func (w *Workspace) Interrupter(ctx context.Context) term.Interrupter {
   130→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   131→}
   132→
   133→// Config returns a copy of the workspace's global configuration.
   134→func (w *Workspace) Config(ctx context.Context) config.Config {
   135→	if w.config == nil {
   136→		c, err := configrpc.FetchConfig(w.conn)
   137→		if err != nil {
   138→			return config.ErrConfig(fmt.Errorf("fetch config: %w", err))
   139→		}
   140→		w.config = c
   141→	}
   142→	return w.config
   143→}
   144→
   145→// RawConn returns the underlying raw connection to the extension host.
   146→// This method should generally not be used by extensions.
   147→func (w *Workspace) RawConn() grpc.ClientConnInterface {
   148→	return w.conn
   149→}
   150→
   151→// NewWorkspace returns a new Workspace. Extensions should use
   152→// ServeWorkspaceExtension, which performs the stdin/stdout exchange
   153→// necessary to receive a valid Config.
   154→func NewWorkspace(req Config, meta Metadata) (*Workspace, error) {
   155→	ret := new(Workspace)
   156→	ret.dataDir = req.DataDir
   157→	opts := []grpc.DialOption{
   158→		grpc.WithContextDialer(
   159→			func(ctx context.Context, _ string) (net.Conn, error) {
   160→				addr, err := net.ResolveUnixAddr("unix", req.Socket)
   161→				if err != nil {
   162→					return nil, err
   163→				}
   164→				var d net.Dialer
   165→				return d.DialContext(ctx, addr.Network(), addr.String())
   166→			},
   167→		)}
   168→
   169→	if len(req.Certificate) != 0 {
   170→		certPool := x509.NewCertPool()
   171→		if !certPool.AppendCertsFromPEM(req.Certificate) {
   172→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("credentials: failed to append certificates")
   173→		}
   174→		tlsCfg := tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true, RootCAs: certPool}
   175→		transportCreds := credentials.NewTLS(&tlsCfg)
   176→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(transportCreds))
   177→	} else {
   178→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(insecure.NewCredentials()))
   179→	}
   180→
   181→	if req.Token != nil {
   182→		tokenSource := oauth2.StaticTokenSource(req.Token)
   183→		rpcCreds := oauth.TokenSource{TokenSource: tokenSource}
   184→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithPerRPCCredentials(rpcCreds))
   185→	}
   186→
   187→	// passthrough is used to indicate that dns
   188→	// resolution SHOULD NOT be performed on target
   189→	conn, err := grpc.NewClient("passthrough:", opts...)
   190→	if err != nil {
   191→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("dial: %w", err)
   192→	}
   193→	ret.conn = conn
   194→	ret.meta = meta
   195→	return ret, nil
   196→}
   197→
   198→func (w *Workspace) editorClient(ctx context.Context) *textrpc.Client {
   199→	if w.editor == nil {
   200→		w.editor = w.Editor(ctx).(*textrpc.Client)
   201→	}
   202→	return w.editor
   203→}
   204→

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

A Go SDK for extending the Rune IDE by building plugins (text-based applications that run inside the Rune IDE's virtual terminal emulator),
or extensions which are programs that interact with Rune via grpc over a local socket.

## Common Commands

```bash
# Build
make              # Build examples and executables
make debug        # Build with race detection

# Testing
make test         # Run all tests with race detector (120s timeout)
make test-no-race # Run tests without race detection
make coverage     # Generate HTML coverage report

# Code Quality
make lint         # Run golangci-lint (600s timeout)
make format       # Format code with go fmt

# Code Generation & License
make generate     # Regenerate protobuf files
make license      # Add Apache 2.0 license headers to Go files

# Pre-commit validation (run before committing)
make generate && make lint && make test
```

## High-Level Architecture

### Component-Handler-TUI Model

The SDK is built around three core abstractions defined in `tui/tui.go`:

1. **Component** - Basic UI element that can be drawn and resized
2. **Handler** - Component that can handle events (keyboard, mouse) and manage cursor/selection
3. **Event Loop** (`tui/run.go`) - Polls tcell events, routes to handlers, redraws, and flushes to terminal

### Package Organization

- **`tui/`** - Core framework interfaces and event loop
- **`term/`** - Terminal I/O abstraction layer (TermboxWriter, VirtualWriter, event types, cursor styles)
- **`component/`** - 48 UI components organized by complexity:
  - Basic: String, Container, Row, Frame, Divider
  - Layout: ResponsiveList, List, FocusList, Grid, FloatingResponsive
  - Behavioral: Background, Overlay, Prompt, Async
- **`handler/`** - Event handling patterns and component wrappers (Nop, Sync, Virtual, Frame, Floating, Responsive, Scrollable)
- **`iterator/`** - Generic iteration utilities with Filter, Map, Reduce, Aggregate operations
- **`api/`** - gRPC-based external integrations:
  - `browserapi/` - Window management, notifications, tabs
  - `workspaceapi/` - URI resolution, file operations, watchers
  - `textapi/` - Editor/text operations
  - `config/` - Configuration management
  - `storageapi/` - Document storage/persistence (largest at ~4000 LOC)
  - `extensionapi/` - Plugin system
  - `schemeapi/` - URI scheme registration

### Key Patterns

**Interface Composition**: Small, focused interfaces combined through composition rather than inheritance.

**Protobuf/gRPC Integration**: API packages contain `*rpc/` subdirectories with:
- `*.proto` - Service definitions
- `*.pb.go` - Generated messages
- `*_grpc.pb.go` - Generated gRPC stubs
- Custom Go implementations wrapping the RPC layer

**Builder Pattern**: Components support zero-value safe constructors (`NewX()`) and full customization (`NewXWithConfig(cfg)`).

**Testing**: Table-driven tests common in component package. Mock RPC types available in `*test/` packages.

## Development Workflow

### Adding New Components

1. Create file in `component/`
2. Implement `tui.Component` interface (and optionally `Responsive`, `Floating`, `WithAttributes`)
3. Provide nop wrapper functions for common handler implementations
4. Add table-driven tests
5. Run `make license` to add Apache 2.0 headers

### Working with APIs

1. Define/modify `.proto` files in `api/<name>/<name>rpc/`
2. Run `make generate` to regenerate Go code
3. Implement service logic in `api/<name>/`
4. Add test helpers in `api/<name>/<name>test/`

### Code Quality Requirements

- Race detector enabled by default in tests
- Pre-commit hooks enforce: YAML validation, protobuf regeneration, license headers
- All tests must pass with `-race -timeout 120s`
- License headers required on all Go files (Apache 2.0)

## Code Conventions

- **Line length**: Maximum 90 columns; wrap lines accordingly.
- **File organization**: Public functions, methods, and types go
  at the top of the file. Private types, functions, and methods
  go at the bottom.
- **Testing**: Always use a table-driven approach.
  - `tui.Component` (and derived) implementations are tested
    using the `comptest` package. See `component/*_test.go`
    for examples.
  - `tui.Handler` (and derived) implementations are tested
    using the `handlertest` package. See `handler/inputbox/`
    and `handler/` for examples.
- **Bugs**: If we find a bug or are fixing one, we must practice
TDD and first add a test that reproduces it, then fix it and verify
that the test passes.

    14→
    15→package extensionapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"crypto/tls"
    20→	"crypto/x509"
    21→	"fmt"
    22→	"net"
    23→
    24→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi"
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/browserapi/browserrpc"
    26→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config"
    27→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config/configrpc"
    28→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi"
    29→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/docmarshal/doctoml"
    30→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/storageapi/storagerpc"
    31→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi"
    32→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/syntaxapi/syntaxrpc"
    33→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi"
    34→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/textapi/textrpc"
    35→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    36→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc"
    37→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/term"
    38→	"golang.org/x/oauth2"
    39→	"google.golang.org/grpc"
    40→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials"
    41→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/insecure"
    42→	"google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/oauth"
    43→)
    44→
    45→// Workspace abstracts resources associated with a Workspace.
    46→type Workspace struct {
    47→	dataDir string
    48→	conn    grpc.ClientConnInterface
    49→	editor  *textrpc.Client
    50→	config  config.Config
    51→	meta    Metadata
    52→}
    53→
    54→// DataDir returns the data directory to store data to re-use across sessions.
    55→func (w *Workspace) DataDir(ctx context.Context) string {
    56→	return w.dataDir
    57→}
    58→
    59→// WindowManager returns the workspace's window manager, which can be used
    60→// to create and manipulate the workspace's browser tabs, windows and bars.
    61→func (w *Workspace) WindowManager(ctx context.Context) browserapi.WindowManager {
    62→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    63→}
    64→
    65→// ResourceOpener returns the workspace's resource opener which allows
    66→// clients to access resources in the workspace. Extensions that need more control
    67→// over the underlying files can use FileSystem instead.
    68→func (w *Workspace) ResourceOpener(ctx context.Context) browserapi.ResourceOpener {
    69→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    70→}
    71→
    72→// Notifications returns the workspace's notifications.
    73→func (w *Workspace) Notifications(ctx context.Context) browserapi.Notifications {
    74→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    75→}
    76→
    77→// FileSystem returns the workspace's file system, which can be used to
    78→// directly manipulate raw files in the workspace. Extensions that
    79→// want to simply open resources as tabs in a workspace should use ResourceOpener.
    80→func (w *Workspace) FileSystem(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.FileSystem {
    81→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    82→}
    83→
    84→// Executor returns the workspace's executor, which can be used
    85→// to start and stop processes.
    86→func (w *Workspace) Executor(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Executor {
    87→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    88→}
    89→
    90→// Terminal returns the workspace's pty capability which can be used to
    91→// implement a terminal.
    92→func (w *Workspace) Terminal(ctx context.Context) workspaceapi.Terminal {
    93→	return workspacerpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
    94→}
    95→
    96→// Editor returns the workspace's editor which can be used to monitor
    97→// and edit files, move the cursor, add location lists with or without
    98→// color attributes, and even set the default background and foreground
    99→// of a resource.
   100→func (w *Workspace) Editor(ctx context.Context) textapi.Editor {
   101→	return textrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   102→}
   103→
   104→// RegisterCommand registers a new command which is to be dispatched to the
   105→// given CommandHandler. This is the primary mechanism extensions should use
   106→// to run new functionality.
   107→func (w *Workspace) RegisterCommand(
   108→	cmd textapi.CommandManual, h textapi.CommandHandler,
   109→) error {
   110→	return w.editorClient(context.Background()).SubscribeCommand(cmd, h)
   111→}
   112→
   113→// Searcher returns the workspace's syntax searcher, which can be used
   114→// to perform AST-level searches across workspace files.
   115→func (w *Workspace) Searcher(ctx context.Context) syntaxapi.Searcher {
   116→	return syntaxrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   117→}
   118→
   119→// Storage returns the workspace's storage facility, which can be used to
   120→// persist extension data across sessions.
   121→func (w *Workspace) Storage(ctx context.Context) storageapi.Service {
   122→	c := new(storagerpc.Client)
   123→	c.Init(w.conn, doctoml.Marshaler())
   124→	return storageapi.WithPartition(c, w.meta.ExtensionID)
   125→}
   126→
   127→// Interrupter returns the workspace's event loop interrupter, which
   128→// can be used to request new draws when state changes asynchronously.
   129→func (w *Workspace) Interrupter(ctx context.Context) term.Interrupter {
   130→	return browserrpc.NewClient(ctx, w.conn)
   131→}
   132→
   133→// Config returns a copy of the workspace's global configuration.
   134→func (w *Workspace) Config(ctx context.Context) config.Config {
   135→	if w.config == nil {
   136→		c, err := configrpc.FetchConfig(w.conn)
   137→		if err != nil {
   138→			return config.ErrConfig(fmt.Errorf("fetch config: %w", err))
   139→		}
   140→		w.config = c
   141→	}
   142→	return w.config
   143→}
   144→
   145→// RawConn returns the underlying raw connection to the extension host.
   146→// This method should generally not be used by extensions.
   147→func (w *Workspace) RawConn() grpc.ClientConnInterface {
   148→	return w.conn
   149→}
   150→
   151→// NewWorkspace returns a new Workspace. Extensions should use
   152→// ServeWorkspaceExtension, which performs the stdin/stdout exchange
   153→// necessary to receive a valid Config.
   154→func NewWorkspace(req Config, meta Metadata) (*Workspace, error) {
   155→	ret := new(Workspace)
   156→	ret.dataDir = req.DataDir
   157→	opts := []grpc.DialOption{
   158→		grpc.WithContextDialer(
   159→			func(ctx context.Context, _ string) (net.Conn, error) {
   160→				addr, err := net.ResolveUnixAddr("unix", req.Socket)
   161→				if err != nil {
   162→					return nil, err
   163→				}
   164→				var d net.Dialer
   165→				return d.DialContext(ctx, addr.Network(), addr.String())
   166→			},
   167→		)}
   168→
   169→	if len(req.Certificate) != 0 {
   170→		certPool := x509.NewCertPool()
   171→		if !certPool.AppendCertsFromPEM(req.Certificate) {
   172→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("credentials: failed to append certificates")
   173→		}
   174→		tlsCfg := tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true, RootCAs: certPool}
   175→		transportCreds := credentials.NewTLS(&tlsCfg)
   176→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(transportCreds))
   177→	} else {
   178→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithTransportCredentials(insecure.NewCredentials()))
   179→	}
   180→
   181→	if req.Token != nil {
   182→		tokenSource := oauth2.StaticTokenSource(req.Token)
   183→		rpcCreds := oauth.TokenSource{TokenSource: tokenSource}
   184→		opts = append(opts, grpc.WithPerRPCCredentials(rpcCreds))
   185→	}
   186→
   187→	// passthrough is used to indicate that dns
   188→	// resolution SHOULD NOT be performed on target
   189→	conn, err := grpc.NewClient("passthrough:", opts...)
   190→	if err != nil {
   191→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("dial: %w", err)
   192→	}
   193→	ret.conn = conn
   194→	ret.meta = meta
   195→	return ret, nil
   196→}
   197→
   198→func (w *Workspace) editorClient(ctx context.Context) *textrpc.Client {
   199→	if w.editor == nil {
   200→		w.editor = w.Editor(ctx).(*textrpc.Client)
   201→	}
   202→	return w.editor
   203→}

    14→
    15→//revive:disable:exported
    16→package schemeapi
    17→
    18→import (
    19→	"context"
    20→	"errors"
    21→	"io"
    22→	"io/fs"
    23→	"syscall"
    24→
    25→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/config"
    26→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/api/workspaceapi"
    27→)
    28→
    29→var (
    30→	// ErrSchemeAlreadyRegistered is retured by RegisterScheme when a scheme for the given
    31→	// uri has already been registered.
    32→	ErrSchemeAlreadyRegistered = errors.New("scheme already registered")
    33→)
    34→
    35→// Scheme abstracts internal workspace scheme implementations.
    36→type Scheme interface {
    37→	FileSystem
    38→	Executor
    39→	Terminal
    40→
    41→	URI(path string) (workspaceapi.URI, error)
    42→	NewFile(fd uintptr, name string) workspaceapi.File
    43→	// Chroot returns a new filesystem from the same type where the new root is
    44→	// the given path.
    45→	Chroot(path string) (Scheme, error)
    46→	// Root returns the root path of the filesystem.
    47→	Root() string
    48→
    49→	Watch(path string, c chan<- EventInfo, events ...Event) (int, error)
    50→	StopWatch(int) error
    51→}
    52→
    53→// FileSystem abstracts the ability to manipulate the file system.
    54→type FileSystem interface {
    55→	// Create creates the named file with mode 0666 (before umask), truncating
    56→	// it if it already exists. If successful, methods on the returned File can
    57→	// be used for I/O; the associated file descriptor has mode O_RDWR.
    58→	Create(filename string) (workspaceapi.File, error)
    59→	// Open opens the named file for reading. If successful, methods on the
    60→	// returned file can be used for reading; the associated file descriptor has
    61→	// mode O_RDONLY.
    62→	Open(filename string) (workspaceapi.File, error)
    63→	// OpenFile is the generalized open call; most users will use Open or Create
    64→	// instead. It opens the named file with specified flag (O_RDONLY etc.) and
    65→	// perm, (0666 etc.) if applicable. If successful, methods on the returned
    66→	// File can be used for I/O.
    67→	OpenFile(filename string, flag int, perm fs.FileMode) (workspaceapi.File, error)
    68→	// Stat returns a FileInfo describing the named file.
    69→	Stat(filename string) (fs.FileInfo, error)
    70→	// Rename renames (moves) oldpath to newpath. If newpath already exists and
    71→	// is not a directory, Rename replaces it. OS-specific restrictions may
    72→	// apply when oldpath and newpath are in different directories.
    73→	Rename(oldpath, newpath string) error
    74→	// Remove removes the named file or directory.
    75→	Remove(filename string) error
    76→	// Join joins any number of path elements into a single path, adding a
    77→	// Separator if necessary. Join calls filepath.Clean on the result; in
    78→	// particular, all empty strings are ignored. On Windows, the result is a
    79→	// UNC path if and only if the first path element is a UNC path.
    80→	Join(elem ...string) string
    81→	// TempFile creates a new temporary file in the directory dir with a name
    82→	// beginning with prefix, opens the file for reading and writing, and
    83→	// returns the resulting File. If dir is the empty string, TempFile
    84→	// uses the default directory for temporary files (see os.TempDir).
    85→	// Multiple programs calling TempFile simultaneously will not choose the
    86→	// same file. The caller can use f.Name() to find the pathname of the file.
    87→	// It is the caller's responsibility to remove the file when no longer
    88→	// needed.
    89→	TempFile(dir, prefix string) (workspaceapi.File, error)
    90→	// Lstat returns a FileInfo describing the named file. If the file is a
    91→	// symbolic link, the returned FileInfo describes the symbolic link. Lstat
    92→	// makes no attempt to follow the link.
    93→	Lstat(filename string) (fs.FileInfo, error)
    94→	// Symlink creates a symbolic-link from link to target. target may be an
    95→	// absolute or relative path, and need not refer to an existing node.
    96→	// Parent directories of link are created as necessary.
    97→	Symlink(oldname, newname string) error
    98→	// Readlink returns the target path of link.
    99→	Readlink(link string) (string, error)
   100→	// ReadDir reads the directory named by dirname and returns a list of
   101→	// directory entries sorted by filename.
   102→	ReadDir(path string) ([]fs.DirEntry, error)
   103→	// MkdirAll creates a directory named path, along with any necessary
   104→	// parents, and returns nil, or else returns an error. The permission bits
   105→	// perm are used for all directories that MkdirAll creates. If path is/
   106→	// already a directory, MkdirAll does nothing and returns nil.
   107→	MkdirAll(filename string, perm fs.FileMode) error
   108→}
   109→
   110→// Event represents the type of filesystem action.
   111→type Event uint32
   112→
   113→// Create, Remove, Write and Rename are the only event values guaranteed to be
   114→// present on all platforms.
   115→const (
   116→	Create Event = iota
   117→	Remove
   118→	Write
   119→	Rename
   120→)
   121→
   122→// AllEvents returns a slice with all permutations of Event.
   123→func AllEvents() []Event {
   124→	return []Event{Create, Remove, Write, Rename}
   125→}
   126→
   127→// String implements fmt.Stringer interface.
   128→func (e Event) String() string {
   129→	switch e {
   130→	case Create:
   131→		return "create"
   132→	case Remove:
   133→		return "remove"
   134→	case Write:
   135→		return "write"
   136→	case Rename:
   137→		return "rename"
   138→	default:
   139→		panic("uknown event")
   140→	}
   141→}
   142→
   143→// EventInfo describes an event reported by Scheme.Watch.
   144→type EventInfo interface {
   145→	// Event is one of the reported events.
   146→	Event() Event
   147→	// URI is the uri of the resource.
   148→	URI() workspaceapi.URI
   149→	// IsDir returns true if event is from a directory.
   150→	IsDir() (bool, error)
   151→}
   152→
   153→// Terminal abstracts the ability to manage pseudoterminals.
   154→type Terminal interface {
   155→	// NewPty creates a new pseudoterminal.
   156→	// The provided context is used to kill the process (by calling
   157→	// os.Process.Kill) if the context becomes done before the command completes on
   158→	// its own.
   159→	NewPty(context.Context) (workspaceapi.Pty, error)
   160→
   161→	// SetPtySize sets the width and height in columns and rows of
   162→	// a pseudoterminal.
   163→	SetPtySize(p workspaceapi.Pty, width, height int) error
   164→}
   165→
   166→// Executor is the public facing API of a workspace's command execution.
   167→type Executor interface {
   168→	// StartCommand starts the given cmd and returns the Pid of the underlying
   169→	// process. The provided context is used to kill the process (by calling
   170→	// os.Process.Kill) if the context.Done channel is closed  before the command
   171→	// completes on its own.
   172→	// Implementations must clean all resources associated with a command
   173→	// once the process exits.
   174→	StartCommand(ctx context.Context, cmd workspaceapi.Cmd) (workspaceapi.Pid, error)
   175→
   176→	// Signal sends a signal to the running process.
   177→	Signal(workspaceapi.Pid, syscall.Signal) error
   178→
   179→	io.Closer
   180→}
   181→
   182→// SchemeFunc represents a Scheme constructor.
   183→type SchemeFunc func(context.Context, config.Config, workspaceapi.URI) (Scheme, error)
   184→
   185→// SchemeManager abstracts the ability to register new URI schemes.
   186→type SchemeManager interface {
   187→	RegisterScheme(string, SchemeFunc) error
   188→}
   189→

    15→package tui
    16→

    18→	"context"
    19→	"strconv"
    20→
    21→	"github.com/unstablebuild/rune-go-sdk/term"
    22→)
    23→
    24→// ContextWithIteration returns a new Context that holds the iteration number i as a payload.
    25→func ContextWithIteration(ctx context.Context, i int64) context.Context {
    26→	return term.ContextWithPayload(ctx, []byte(strconv.FormatInt(i, 10)))
    27→}
    28→
    29→// IterationFromContext returns the ID value stored in ctx, if any.
    30→func IterationFromContext(ctx context.Context) (int64, bool) {
    31→	payload, ok := term.PayloadFromContext(ctx)
    32→	if !ok {
    33→		return 0, false
    34→	}
    35→	return IterationFromRawBytes(payload)
    36→}
    37→
    38→// IterationFromRawBytes parses the given payload into an iteration number,
    39→// as formatted by ContextWithIteration.
    40→func IterationFromRawBytes(payload []byte) (int64, bool) {
    41→	i, err := strconv.ParseInt(string(payload), 10, 64)
    42→	if err != nil {
    43→		return 0, false
    44→	}
    45→	return i, true
    46→}
    47→

   333→func TestLooksLikeURI(t *testing.T) {
   334→	tests := []struct {
   335→		name string
   336→		arg  string
   337→		want bool
   338→	}{
   339→		{
   340→			name: "file_uri",
   341→			arg:  "file:///tmp/test.txt",
   342→			want: true,
   343→		},
   344→		{
   345→			name: "http_uri",
   346→			arg:  "http://example.com/path",
   347→			want: true,
   348→		},
   349→		{
   350→			name: "https_uri",
   351→			arg:  "https://example.com/path",
   352→			want: true,
   353→		},
   354→		{
   355→			name: "custom_scheme",
   356→			arg:  "rune://workspace/file.txt",
   357→			want: true,
   358→		},
   359→		{
   360→			name: "absolute_path",
   361→			arg:  "/tmp/test.txt",
   362→			want: false,
   363→		},
   364→		{
   365→			name: "relative_path",
   366→			arg:  "./test.txt",
   367→			want: false,
   368→		},
   369→		{
   370→			name: "relative_path_no_dot",
   371→			arg:  "test.txt",
   372→			want: false,
   373→		},
   374→		{
   375→			name: "windows_path",
   376→			arg:  "C:\\Users\\test.txt",
   377→			want: false,
   378→		},
   379→		{
   380→			name: "empty_string",
   381→			arg:  "",
   382→			want: false,
   383→		},
   384→	}
   385→	for _, tt := range tests {
   386→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   387→			got := looksLikeURI(tt.arg)
   388→			require.Equal(t, tt.want, got)
   389→		})
   390→	}
   391→}

   393→func TestResolveURIArgWithURI(t *testing.T) {
   394→	tests := []struct {
   395→		name    string
   396→		arg     string
   397→		wantURI string
   398→		wantErr bool
   399→	}{
   400→		{
   401→			name:    "file_uri",
   402→			arg:     "file:///tmp/test.txt",
   403→			wantURI: "file:///tmp/test.txt",
   404→		},
   405→		{
   406→			name:    "rune_uri",
   407→			arg:     "rune://workspace/src/main.go",
   408→			wantURI: "rune://workspace/src/main.go",
   409→		},
   410→		{
   411→			name:    "uri_with_query",
   412→			arg:     "file:///tmp/test.txt?line=10",
   413→			wantURI: "file:///tmp/test.txt?line=10",
   414→		},
   415→		{
   416→			name:    "invalid_uri",
   417→			arg:     "://invalid",
   418→			wantErr: true,
   419→		},
   420→	}
   421→	for _, tt := range tests {
   422→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   423→			a := &app{}
   424→			got, err := a.resolveURIArg(
   425→				context.Background(), tt.arg,
   426→			)
   427→			if tt.wantErr {
   428→				require.Error(t, err)
   429→				return
   430→			}
   431→			require.NoError(t, err)
   432→			require.Equal(t, tt.wantURI, got.String())
   433→		})
   434→	}
   435→}

  1766→	browserrpc.RegisterNotificationsServer(

  1770→	browserrpc.RegisterResourceOpenerServer(
  1771→		srv, opener,
  1772→	)
  1773→	workspacerpc.RegisterSchemeServer(srv, scheme)

  1775→	docpb.RegisterDocumentStoreServer(srv, ds)
  1776→	syntaxrpc.RegisterSyntaxServer(srv, syn)

  1780→	t.Setenv("RUNE_SOCKET", sockPath)
  1781→	t.Setenv("RUNE_DATADIR", datadir)
  1782→
  1783→	return &testEnv{

  1786→		opener: opener, scheme: scheme,
  1787→		editor: ed, docStore: ds, syntax: syn,
  1788→		cleanup: func() { srv.Stop() },
  1789→	}
  1790→}
  1791→
  1792→func (e *testEnv) run(

  1796→
  1797→	root := newRootCmd()

  1799→
  1800→	old := os.Stdout
  1801→	r, ww, err := os.Pipe()
  1802→	require.NoError(e.t, err)
  1803→	os.Stdout = ww
  1804→
  1805→	runErr := root.ExecuteContext(context.Background())
  1806→
  1807→	_ = ww.Close()
  1808→	os.Stdout = old
  1809→
  1810→	var buf bytes.Buffer
  1811→	_, _ = buf.ReadFrom(r)
  1812→	_ = r.Close()
  1813→
  1814→	return buf.String(), runErr
  1815→}

Perfect. Now let me create a comprehensive report of my findings. Based on my exploration, I have gathered all the critical information about process execution and terminal capabilities in the workspaceapi.

## Summary

I've completed a thorough exploration of the workspaceapi process execution and terminal capabilities. Here's my comprehensive report:

### 1. Service Definitions (workspace.proto)

**Executor Service** - Handles process execution via bidirectional streaming:
- `StartCommand(stream CommandPayload) returns (stream CommandPayload)` - Bidirectional stream for process lifecycle management
- `Signal(SignalRequest) returns (SignalResponse)` - Send signals to running processes

**Terminal Service** - Manages pseudoterminals:
- `NewPty(NewPtyRequest) returns (NewPtyResponse)` - Create new PTY sessions
- `SetPtySize(SetPtySizeRequest) returns (SetPtySizeResponse)` - Configure PTY dimensions

### 2. Command Startup and Stream Protocol

**StartCommandRequest fields** (lines 146-162 in proto):
```
- name: Executable name/path
- dir: Working directory  
- args: Command arguments (repeated)
- env: Environment variables (repeated)
- stdin/stdout/stderr: Boolean flags indicating if streams are needed
- stdin_fd/stdout_fd/stderr_fd: File descriptor numbers for redirection to files
- stdin_name/stdout_name/stderr_name: Filenames for redirection
- setsid: Session ID flag
- setctty: Control TTY flag
```

**CommandPayload protocol** (lines 238-267):
- `TypeStart` - Initial request with StartCommandRequest
- `TypeStarted` - Response with PID (int64)
- `TypeIO` - Data stream messages (stdin/stdout/stderr with bytes)
- `TypeDone` - Completion with optional exit_error
- `TypeError` - Error during execution

### 3. How Commands Are Started

**Client-side flow** (`workspacerpc/client.go` lines 312-405):
1. Opens bidirectional stream to `Executor.StartCommand`
2. Sends `CommandPayload` with type `TypeStart` containing `StartCommandRequest`
3. Waits for `TypeStarted` message containing the process PID
4. Spawns goroutines to stream stdin and handle stdout/stderr in parallel
5. Passes control to `streamCommandData()` which handles bidirectional I/O until completion

**Key streaming implementation** (`workspacerpc/command_stream.go`):
- `waitForPid()` (lines 58-71): Blocks until receiving Started message
- `streamStdin()` (lines 73-124): Reads from cmd.Stdin in 64KB chunks, sends via CommandPayload
- `streamCommandData()` (lines 126-225): Main loop receiving stdout/stderr, forwarding to cmd.Stdout/cmd.Stderr

**I/O Redirection Support** (lines 349-351 in client.go):
```go
req.Start.StdinFd, req.Start.StdinName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stdin)
req.Start.StdoutFd, req.Start.StdoutName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stdout)
req.Start.StderrFd, req.Start.StderrName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stderr)
```
Can redirect to local files or stream over RPC - server handles both cases.

### 4. PTY Session Management

**PTY Creation** (`workspace.proto` lines 198-206):
```
NewPtyResponse contains:
- master: Master device file path
- master_fd: Master file descriptor
- slave: Slave device file path
- slave_fd: Slave file descriptor
```

**PTY Size Control** (`workspace.proto` lines 208-215):
```
SetPtySizeRequest specifies:
- width/height: Columns and rows
- Both master and slave identifiers (path and fd pairs)
```

**PTY Integration** (`client.go` lines 421-463):
- `StartPty()` creates new PTY via Terminal service
- Returns wrapped `FileClient` objects for master and slave
- `SetPtySize()` configures dimensions via Terminal service

### 5. Process Control Mechanisms

**Signal Handling** (`client.go` lines 407-418):
- `Signal(Pid, syscall.Signal)` method accepts any Unix signal
- Sends `SignalRequest` with PID and signal number (int32)
- No filtering of signals at client level

**Session/TTY Control flags** (lines 330-346 in client.go):
```go
var setsid, setctty bool
if cmd.SysProcAttr != nil {
    setsid = cmd.SysProcAttr.Setsid
    setctty = cmd.SysProcAttr.Setctty
}
```
These are passed through to server, but comment at line 63 in workspace.go notes:
```
// SysProcAttr is ignored if passed from a extension.
```

### 6. Security Checks and Sandboxing

**Currently implemented:**
- **Minimal sandbox/security checks** - No evidence of explicit restrictions
- **File descriptor validation** - `FileClient.tryUnwrapFile()` validates file handles
- **Error handling** - Type-safe error responses (exist/permission errors)
- **Root context** - Client has optional `root` field suggesting chroot capability but not enforced
- **Context timeouts** - 5-second default timeout for non-streaming operations
- **Watcher timeout** - 2-minute timeout when delivering results to ProcessWatcher (line 31, command_stream.go)

**What is NOT implemented:**
- No command execution whitelist/blacklist
- No argument validation or sanitization
- No environment variable filtering
- No path restriction (beyond optional root parameter)
- No resource limits (CPU, memory, file handles)
- No capability dropping
- No seccomp/pledge restrictions
- `SysProcAttr` is explicitly ignored for extensions (comment in workspace.go line 63)

### 7. Streaming Architecture

**Bidirectional Design:**
- Client and server exchange `CommandPayload` messages continuously
- Stdin streaming happens on separate goroutine (line 131 in command_stream.go)
- Stdout/stderr received in same loop as control messages
- Both client sends and receives in parallel

**Buffer Management:**
- 64KB read buffer for stdin (line 29 in command_stream.go)
- No apparent buffer limits for stdout/stderr - streamed as received
- Data validation: server response checks `len(data) <= len(p)` (line 90 in file_client.go)

**Process Lifecycle:**
- Starts with TypeStart message
- Sends Started with PID after process spawns
- Streams IO until process exits
- Concludes with Done message containing exit error
- 2-minute timeout for watcher callback (line 206-207 in command_stream.go)

### 8. File Path Locations

- **Proto definitions**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/workspace.proto`
- **Interface definitions**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspace.go`
- **Client implementation**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/client.go`
- **Stream handling**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/command_stream.go`
- **gRPC stubs**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/workspace_grpc.pb.go`
- **Scheme API (interfaces)**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/schemeapi/scheme.go`

### Critical Findings for Sandbox Integration

1. **Interception points**: The sandbox should intercept at the `ExecutorServer.StartCommand()` implementation, not the client side
2. **Command metadata available**: Full command path, arguments, environment, and working directory are transmitted in StartCommandRequest
3. **No built-in restrictions**: The architecture provides no sandboxing - all validation must be external
4. **PTY support required**: Full PTY session management for interactive shells with proper size negotiation
5. **Signal handling**: Must wrap `Signal()` to control which signals can be sent to which processes
6. **File descriptor awareness**: Server-side can use file descriptors or file names - sandbox must handle both

**Terminal Service** - Manages pseudoterminals:

- `SetPtySize(SetPtySizeRequest) returns (SetPtySizeResponse)` - Configure PTY dimensions

### 2. Command Startup and Stream Protocol

**StartCommandRequest fields** (lines 146-162 in proto):
```
- name: Executable name/path
- dir: Working directory  
- args: Command arguments (repeated)
- env: Environment variables (repeated)
- stdin/stdout/stderr: Boolean flags indicating if streams are needed
- stdin_fd/stdout_fd/stderr_fd: File descriptor numbers for redirection to files
- stdin_name/stdout_name/stderr_name: Filenames for redirection
- setsid: Session ID flag
- setctty: Control TTY flag
```

**CommandPayload protocol** (lines 238-267):
- `TypeStart` - Initial request with StartCommandRequest
- `TypeStarted` - Response with PID (int64)
- `TypeIO` - Data stream messages (stdin/stdout/stderr with bytes)
- `TypeDone` - Completion with optional exit_error
- `TypeError` - Error during execution

### 3. How Commands Are Started

**Client-side flow** (`workspacerpc/client.go` lines 312-405):
1. Opens bidirectional stream to `Executor.StartCommand`
2. Sends `CommandPayload` with type `TypeStart` containing `StartCommandRequest`
3. Waits for `TypeStarted` message containing the process PID
4. Spawns goroutines to stream stdin and handle stdout/stderr in parallel
5. Passes control to `streamCommandData()` which handles bidirectional I/O until completion

**Key streaming implementation** (`workspacerpc/command_stream.go`):
- `waitForPid()` (lines 58-71): Blocks until receiving Started message
- `streamStdin()` (lines 73-124): Reads from cmd.Stdin in 64KB chunks, sends via CommandPayload
- `streamCommandData()` (lines 126-225): Main loop receiving stdout/stderr, forwarding to cmd.Stdout/cmd.Stderr

**I/O Redirection Support** (lines 349-351 in client.go):
```go
req.Start.StdinFd, req.Start.StdinName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stdin)
req.Start.StdoutFd, req.Start.StdoutName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stdout)
req.Start.StderrFd, req.Start.StderrName = tryUnwrapFile(cmd.Stderr)
```
Can redirect to local files or stream over RPC - server handles both cases.

### 4. PTY Session Management

**PTY Creation** (`workspace.proto` lines 198-206):
```
NewPtyResponse contains:
- master: Master device file path
- master_fd: Master file descriptor
- slave: Slave device file path
- slave_fd: Slave file descriptor
```

**PTY Size Control** (`workspace.proto` lines 208-215):
```
SetPtySizeRequest specifies:
- width/height: Columns and rows
- Both master and slave identifiers (path and fd pairs)
```

**PTY Integration** (`client.go` lines 421-463):
- `StartPty()` creates new PTY via Terminal service
- Returns wrapped `FileClient` objects for master and slave
- `SetPtySize()` configures dimensions via Terminal service

### 5. Process Control Mechanisms

**Signal Handling** (`client.go` lines 407-418):
- `Signal(Pid, syscall.Signal)` method accepts any Unix signal
- Sends `SignalRequest` with PID and signal number (int32)
- No filtering of signals at client level

**Session/TTY Control flags** (lines 330-346 in client.go):
```go
var setsid, setctty bool
if cmd.SysProcAttr != nil {
    setsid = cmd.SysProcAttr.Setsid
    setctty = cmd.SysProcAttr.Setctty
}
```
These are passed through to server, but comment at line 63 in workspace.go notes:
```
// SysProcAttr is ignored if passed from a extension.
```

### 6. Security Checks and Sandboxing

**Currently implemented:**
- **Minimal sandbox/security checks** - No evidence of explicit restrictions
- **File descriptor validation** - `FileClient.tryUnwrapFile()` validates file handles
- **Error handling** - Type-safe error responses (exist/permission errors)
- **Root context** - Client has optional `root` field suggesting chroot capability but not enforced
- **Context timeouts** - 5-second default timeout for non-streaming operations
- **Watcher timeout** - 2-minute timeout when delivering results to ProcessWatcher (line 31, command_stream.go)

**What is NOT implemented:**
- No command execution whitelist/blacklist
- No argument validation or sanitization
- No environment variable filtering
- No path restriction (beyond optional root parameter)
- No resource limits (CPU, memory, file handles)
- No capability dropping
- No seccomp/pledge restrictions
- `SysProcAttr` is explicitly ignored for extensions (comment in workspace.go line 63)

### 7. Streaming Architecture

**Bidirectional Design:**
- Client and server exchange `CommandPayload` messages continuously
- Stdin streaming happens on separate goroutine (line 131 in command_stream.go)
- Stdout/stderr received in same loop as control messages
- Both client sends and receives in parallel

**Buffer Management:**
- 64KB read buffer for stdin (line 29 in command_stream.go)
- No apparent buffer limits for stdout/stderr - streamed as received
- Data validation: server response checks `len(data) <= len(p)` (line 90 in file_client.go)

**Process Lifecycle:**
- Starts with TypeStart message
- Sends Started with PID after process spawns
- Streams IO until process exits
- Concludes with Done message containing exit error
- 2-minute timeout for watcher callback (line 206-207 in command_stream.go)

### 8. File Path Locations

- **Proto definitions**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/workspace.proto`
- **Interface definitions**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspace.go`
- **Client implementation**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/client.go`
- **Stream handling**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/command_stream.go`
- **gRPC stubs**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/workspaceapi/workspacerpc/workspace_grpc.pb.go`
- **Scheme API (interfaces)**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/schemeapi/scheme.go`

### Critical Findings for Sandbox Integration

1. **Interception points**: The sandbox should intercept at the `ExecutorServer.StartCommand()` implementation, not the client side
2. **Command metadata available**: Full command path, arguments, environment, and working directory are transmitted in StartCommandRequest
3. **No built-in restrictions**: The architecture provides no sandboxing - all validation must be external
4. **PTY support required**: Full PTY session management for interactive shells with proper size negotiation
5. **Signal handling**: Must wrap `Signal()` to control which signals can be sent to which processes
6. **File descriptor awareness**: Server-side can use file descriptors or file names - sandbox must handle both

## Summary: Plugin vs Extension Architecture in Rune Go SDK

Based on my exploration of the codebase, here's how plugins and extensions work differently and how sandbox execution models would apply:

### 1. **TUI/Plugin Architecture (Component-Handler-TUI Model)**

**Location**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/tui/tui.go` and `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/tui/run.go`

**Core Interfaces**:
- **Component** (`tui.go`, lines 23-33): Basic UI element with:
  - `Resize(width, height int)` - Define available space
  - `Draw(term.Writer)` - Render to terminal

- **Handler** (`tui.go`, lines 35-51): Interactive component with:
  - `Handle(term.Event) (exit, handled bool)` - Process keyboard/mouse events
  - `Cursor() (coordinates, style, show bool)` - Manage cursor state
  - `Selection() (string, bool)` - Track text selection

**Event Loop** (`run.go`, lines 30-193):
- `Run(root Handler, opts ...Option)` blocks and manages full event loop
- Polls tcell events via `term.Poll()` (line 125)
- Redraws on every iteration (lines 138, 173-177)
- Handles OS interrupt signals with double-tap exit (lines 145, 109-115)
- Executes all rendering/event handling locally in the calling process

### 2. **Term Package - Terminal I/O Abstraction**

**Location**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/term/`

**Key Components**:
- **Writer Interface** (`structures.go`, lines 101-115):
  ```go
  type Writer interface {
    Context() context.Context
    SetCell(Coordinates, Cell)
    UnionAttributes(Coordinates, Attributes)
  }
  ```

- **TermboxWriter** (`termbox_writer.go`): Direct terminal output via tcell/termbox
  - Direct screen manipulation with `SetCell()`, `Flush()`
  - Uses tcell library for cross-platform terminal control

- **Event Types** (`structures.go`, lines 65-81):
  - EventKey, EventResize, EventError, EventInterrupt
  - Mouse coordinates, raw bytes, custom context

### 3. **Extension Architecture (Workspace Model)**

**Location**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/extensionapi/`

**Communication Protocol** (`serve.go`, lines 86-165):

Extensions use **2-phase JSON handshake over stdio**:
1. Extension writes Metadata JSON to stdout (line 103)
2. Host sends Config JSON (with socket path) to stdin (line 108-114)
3. Extension establishes gRPC connection via Unix socket (line 189)

**WorkspaceExtension Interface** (`serve.go`, lines 47-51):
```go
type WorkspaceExtension interface {
  ExtendWorkspace(context.Context, *Workspace, config.Config) error
}
```

**Workspace provides gRPC access to** (`workspace.go`, lines 45-196):
- `WindowManager` - Browser tabs/windows
- `FileSystem` - Workspace files
- `Executor` - Process management
- `Terminal` - PTY access
- `Editor` - Text editing operations
- `Searcher` - AST-level searches
- `Storage` - Persistent data (partitioned by extension ID)
- `Interrupter` - Request redraws from `term.Interrupter`

### 4. **Key Execution Model Differences**

| Aspect | Plugin (TUI) | Extension |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Execution** | Runs in Rune's process, owns event loop | Separate process, passive listener |
| **Terminal I/O** | Direct via tcell/termbox | Requests terminal via gRPC (Terminal API) |
| **Communication** | Local function calls | gRPC over Unix socket + OAuth2 tokens |
| **Lifecycle** | `tui.Run()` blocks; exits on Escape or double-tap Ctrl-C | Runs until signal (SIGINT/SIGTERM) |
| **Data Persistence** | File system only | Partitioned storage API with encryption |
| **Resource Access** | Limited; must use APIs | gRPC APIs for files, editor, syntax, etc. |
| **Sandboxing** | Process-level (runs with Rune) | Process isolation + gRPC with token authentication |

### 5. **Example Plugin Implementation**

**Location**: `/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/examples/dialogue/main.go`

This is a complete TUI plugin example:
- Implements `Handler` interface with custom message display
- Uses `InputBox` handler for user input
- Calls `tui.Run(demo)` to start event loop (line 150)
- Direct component composition (Container, Row, ResponsiveString)
- Handles events locally: processes Enter, updates state, requests redraw

### 6. **Sandboxing Implications**

**For Plugins**:
- Run in-process with Rune IDE - NO isolation
- Full access to Rune's memory, file descriptors, terminal
- Must be trusted code compiled with Rune
- Crash/hang can crash entire IDE
- Good for: Custom UI components, performance-critical code

**For Extensions**:
- Separate process with gRPC boundary
- Access controlled via OAuth2 tokens
- Permission system defined in Metadata (`permissions.go`)
- Process can be killed independently
- Data stored in DataDir (partitioned by ExtensionID)
- Good for: Untrusted code, plugins from unknown sources, hot-reload

### 7. **Event Loop Architecture Detail**

The event loop (`run.go`, lines 117-193):
1. Initializes terminal with `term.Init()` (line 34)
2. Resizes root handler to full screen (lines 122-123)
3. Polls events in tight loop with interrupt batching (lines 142-189)
4. Conflates duplicate interrupt events to prevent abuse (lines 93-103)
5. Context tracks iteration number for interrupt disambiguation (lines 134, 186)
6. Supports custom user functions via `PublishEvent()`'s UserFunc field (line 165)

### Key Files Summary:

- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/tui/tui.go`** - Component/Handler interfaces
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/tui/run.go`** - Event loop implementation
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/term/structures.go`** - Writer interface, event types
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/term/termbox_writer.go`** - tcell-based terminal I/O
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/extensionapi/serve.go`** - Extension handshake protocol
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/api/extensionapi/workspace.go`** - gRPC resource access
- **`/Users/atzarisurinachcorominas/.rune/worktrees/sandbox/examples/dialogue/main.go`** - Plugin example

The SDK enables two distinct extension patterns with fundamentally different sandboxing properties suitable for different trust and performance requirements.

content.thinking:
Excellent! I now have comprehensive information from all three exploration agents. Let me synthesize what I've learned:

## Key Findings Summary

### Extension Architecture
1. Extensions run as separate processes, communicate via JSON over stdin/stdout for initial handshake
2. Then use gRPC over Unix socket with OAuth2 tokens for API access
3. Permission system exists (12 permission types) but enforcement happens on the host side
4. Extensions declare permissions in Metadata, host grants/denies

### Plugin Architecture
1. Plugins run in-process with Rune IDE (no isolation)
2. Direct terminal access via tcell/termbox
3. Must be trusted code - crash can crash IDE

### Process Execution (Critical for Sandbox)
1. Executor service uses bidirectional streaming for command lifecycle
2. Full command info available: path, args, env, working dir
3. **NO built-in sandboxing** - all validation must be external
4. PTY support for interactive shells
5. Signal handling available
6. `SysProcAttr` is explicitly ignored for extensions

### Sandbox Integration Points
1. **Extension launch**: Host can run extension binary in sandbox VM
2. **gRPC interception**: Host controls the Unix socket and can proxy/filter API calls
3. **Executor service**: Must be intercepted/proxied to enforce command restrictions
4. **File system access**: FileSystem API can be proxied

Now I need to design the sandbox architecture. Let me think about the approaches:

## Architecture Options

### Option 1: Matchlock-Based Sandbox
- Use Matchlock to run extension binaries in microVMs
- Extension inside VM connects to a gRPC proxy exposed via vsock
- Proxy on host side enforces policies and forwards to real Rune APIs
- Pros: True VM isolation, Matchlock already handles VM lifecycle
- Cons: Requires KVM on Linux, may have startup overhead

### Option 2: Native Sandbox (No VM)


- Leverage OS primitives: Linux namespaces and seccomp, macOS sandbox profiles
- Run extension process with restricted syscalls and capabilities
- Proxy gRPC calls through host-side policy enforcement
- Pros: Lower latency, no hypervisor dependency
- Cons: Weaker isolation guarantees, platform-specific implementation

### Option 3: Hybrid Approach
- Deploy VM isolation for untrusted extensions (maximum security)
- Use native OS sandboxing for vetted code (better performance)
- Let administrators configure the isolation tier per extension

The user's emphasis on defense-in-depth and preventing escapes suggests they're prioritizing maximum isolation strength over performance, which points toward the VM-based approach.

## Platform Strategy

For Matchlock's supported platforms:
- **Linux x86_64/arm64**: Firecracker microVMs with KVM
- **macOS arm64**: Apple Virtualization.framework
- **macOS x86_64**: Docker/Lima as secondary option

## Package Structure

The sandbox implementation needs clear separation between public interfaces, gRPC definitions, policy enforcement, and audit logging. The `api/sandboxapi/` directory should contain the core sandbox interface, generated protobuf code, policy definitions for capabilities and permissions, and audit trail functionality.

The runtime layer abstracts platform-specific implementations—Firecracker for Linux, Virtualization.framework for macOS, with a native OS-level fallback. A gRPC proxy sits between the API and runtime, applying per-API interceptors to enforce capability restrictions. Test utilities provide mocks for validation.

Before proceeding, I should clarify the scope of capability restrictions, how audit events should be structured, and whether the native sandbox fallback is necessary for all platforms or just specific use cases.

Excellent! I now have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me summarize the key findings and ask some clarifying questions before designing the final plan.

## Key Findings

### Extension Architecture
- Extensions run as **separate processes** with JSON handshake → gRPC over Unix socket
- **12 permission types** declared in Metadata, but enforcement is host-side
- **No existing sandbox** - extensions have direct process access via Executor API

### Process Execution (Critical Gap)
- Executor service provides full command execution with bidirectional streaming
- **NO built-in sandboxing** - full path, args, env available but unrestricted
- `SysProcAttr` is explicitly ignored for extensions (noted in code)
- PTY support for interactive shells

### Plugin vs Extension
- **Plugins**: Run in-process with Rune, no isolation, must be trusted
- **Extensions**: Separate process, gRPC boundary, can be sandboxed

### Integration Points for Sandbox
1. **Extension launch** - Run extension binary inside sandbox VM
2. **gRPC proxy** - Intercept API calls at Unix socket boundary
3. **Executor interception** - Wrap command execution with policy enforcement
4. **FileSystem proxy** - Filter file operations by policy

Before I finalize the plan, I have some questions:

questions.question: Should the sandbox be used only for extensions (separate processes via gRPC), or do you also want to sandbox plugins (in-process TUI apps)?

options.label: Extensions only (Recommended)
options.description: Sandbox the separate process extensions that communicate via gRPC. Plugins remain trusted in-process code.

options.label: Both extensions and plugins
options.description: Also run plugins in sandbox VMs with terminal I/O proxied. More complex but fully isolated.

options.label: Extensions + optional plugin sandbox
options.description: Primary focus on extensions, with optional VM-based plugin execution for untrusted sources.

questions.question: How should the sandbox integrate with Rune - as a library embedded in Rune, or as a standalone service?

options.label: Library in this SDK (Recommended)
options.description: Sandbox runtime as a Go package in this SDK. Rune imports and uses it to launch sandboxed extensions.

options.label: Standalone daemon/service
options.description: Separate sandbox service that Rune communicates with via IPC. More complex but decoupled.

options.label: CLI tool + library
options.description: Both a CLI tool (like matchlock) for manual use and a library for programmatic integration.

questions.question: What's your preference for the underlying sandbox technology?

options.label: Fork/vendor Matchlock (Recommended)
options.description: Use Matchlock's VM runtime as foundation. Already Go, supports Linux+macOS, MIT licensed.

options.label: Build on Apple Container + Firecracker
options.description: Use Apple Container for macOS, Firecracker directly for Linux. More control but more work.

options.label: Native OS isolation only
options.description: Use namespaces/seccomp on Linux, sandbox profiles on macOS. Faster but weaker isolation.

questions.question: Should the sandbox support running arbitrary OCI container images, or only extension binaries?

options.label: Extension binaries only (Recommended)
options.description: Sandbox runs the extension binary directly in a minimal environment. Simpler, faster startup.

options.label: OCI container images
options.description: Extensions can specify a container image to run in. More flexible but adds complexity.

options.label: Both modes
options.description: Support direct binary execution and optional container image specification.

message.content: This needs to be retrofitted into a workspaceapi.Executor, that's the integration point: clients pass a workspaceapi.Cmd and we need to run that inside a sandbox and manage everything else, keeping the sandboxing transparent for the calling client. How does this affect your evaluation of vendors?

content.thinking:
This is a critical clarification that changes my understanding significantly. Let me re-evaluate:

The user is saying:
1. The integration point is `workspaceapi.Executor` - specifically the `Start(context.Context, *Cmd) error` method
2. Clients pass a `workspaceapi.Cmd` (which has `Name`, `Args`, `Env`, `Dir`, `Stdin`, `Stdout`, `Stderr`, etc.)
3. The sandbox implementation needs to be transparent to the caller - they just call `executor.Start(ctx, cmd)` and it runs sandboxed
4. All the sandbox complexity (VM lifecycle, network policy, resource limits, etc.) should be hidden behind this interface

This is a very different model than what I was thinking! Instead of sandboxing the entire extension process, they want to sandbox individual command executions that extensions request through the Executor API.

This means:
- Extensions run normally (not in VMs)
- When an extension calls `Executor.Start()` to run a command, THAT command runs in a sandbox
- The sandbox wraps individual subprocess executions, not the extension itself

This changes the vendor evaluation significantly:

**Matchlock evaluation:**
- Matchlock is designed to run entire agent sessions in VMs
- It has a CLI like `matchlock run alpine -- echo "hello"`
- It can run long-lived sandboxes that you attach to
- BUT: It's designed for running whole workloads, not individual short commands
- Starting a new VM for each command would have significant overhead (even with sub-second boot)
- However, Matchlock does support long-lived sandboxes that you can exec into

**Apple Container evaluation:**
- Similar to Docker - runs containers
- Each `container run` starts a new container
- Has `container exec` for running commands in existing containers
- Could keep a container running and exec commands into it

**What we actually need:**
1. A way to maintain a sandbox/VM session per... what? Per extension? Per workspace?


2. Execute commands inside that session
3. Stream stdin/stdout/stderr back to the Cmd's writers
4. Handle process signals
5. Manage PTY sessions

The architecture likely involves spawning a sandbox VM or container when an extension first calls Executor, keeping it alive for the extension's lifetime, then executing commands inside via exec or vsock. Alternatively, we could run one shared sandbox per workspace with all extensions' commands isolated via user namespaces or separate directories.

Looking at the `workspaceapi.Cmd` structure, it has fields for the executable name, working directory, arguments, environment variables, and I/O writers, plus a `SysProcAttr` that gets ignored for extensions and a `ProcessWatcher` for callbacks. The `Executor` interface provides `Start()` to begin execution and stream I/O, and `Signal()` to send signals to a running process by PID. For transparent sandboxing, we'd need to implement this interface while routing execution through a sandbox.

Matchlock looks promising since it's Go-based and importable as a library, supports long-lived sandboxes with `start/attach/stop` commands, uses FUSE for workspace mounting over vsock so files are visible in the VM, already handles stdin/stdout streaming, and has built-in secret injection and network allowlisting. The main uncertainty is whether the SDK supports the exec-into-running-sandbox pattern we need, though we could potentially contribute upstream or fork if necessary. Apple Container is officially maintained and supports `container exec` for running commands in existing containers with OCI compatibility, but it's written in a different language.

Firecracker offers low-level control to implement exactly what we need but requires significant work and is Linux-only. gVisor provides strong isolation as a user-space kernel and works as an OCI-compatible container runtime, though it has Linux-only limitations and performance overhead. Native OS isolation through namespaces and seccomp avoids VM overhead with faster startup and easier command wrapping, but has weaker isolation guarantees.

Given the need for a transparent `Executor` interface, I should use Matchlock's sandbox as a library with persistent VMs per extension. On the first execution, spawn the sandbox if needed, then communicate via vsock to a guest agent for running commands and streaming I/O back. For macOS systems without Matchlock, fall back to native sandbox-exec or Docker/Lima as alternatives.

Now I can refine my questions based on this approach.

content.text:
Ah, this is a crucial clarification! You want sandboxing at the **command execution level**, not the extension process level. Let me re-evaluate:

## Revised Understanding

```
Extension Process (runs normally)
    │
    ├─► Calls executor.Start(ctx, &Cmd{Name: "npm", Args: ["install"], ...})
    │
    ▼
Sandboxed Executor (implements workspaceapi.Executor)
    │
    ├─► Spawns/reuses sandbox VM
    ├─► Executes "npm install" INSIDE the sandbox
    ├─► Streams stdin/stdout/stderr back transparently
    └─► Handles Signal() calls
```

## How This Changes Vendor Evaluation

| Concern | Matchlock | Apple Container | Native (namespace/seccomp) |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| **Exec into running sandbox** | ✅ `sandbox attach` + vsock | ✅ `container exec` | ✅ Direct process |
| **Stream I/O back** | ✅ Built-in | ✅ Built-in | ✅ Direct pipes |
| **Go library integration** | ⚠️ CLI-focused, SDK exists | ❌ Swift, CLI only | ✅ Pure Go |
| **Per-command overhead** | ⚠️ Need persistent sandbox | ⚠️ Need persistent container | ✅ Minimal |
| **Filesystem mounting** | ✅ FUSE over vsock | ✅ Volume mounts | ✅ Bind mounts |

## Key Insight

For transparent `Executor` wrapping, we need:
1. **Persistent sandbox session** - can't spin up a new VM per command (too slow)
2. **Exec-into-sandbox** capability - run arbitrary commands in existing sandbox
3. **Bidirectional I/O streaming** - map `Cmd.Stdin/Stdout/Stderr` to sandbox
4. **Signal forwarding** - `Signal(pid, SIGTERM)` must reach sandboxed process
5. **Working directory + env** - honor `Cmd.Dir` and `Cmd.Env` inside sandbox

## Revised Options

**Option A: Matchlock as foundation**
- Keep sandbox VM running per workspace/extension
- Use Matchlock's vsock guest agent for command execution
- Wrap their SDK to implement `workspaceapi.Executor`
- Pros: VM isolation, existing infra
- Cons: Need to adapt CLI-focused design to library use

**Option B: Build custom executor with VM backends**
- Implement `Executor` interface directly
- Use Firecracker (Linux) / Virtualization.framework (macOS) for VM lifecycle
- Build minimal guest agent for command execution over vsock
- Pros: Full control, optimized for this use case
- Cons: More implementation effort

**Option C: Container-based (Docker/containerd)**
- Use containers instead of VMs
- Keep container running, exec commands into it
- Pros: Familiar, mature tooling, OCI images
- Cons: Weaker isolation than VMs, dependency on Docker daemon

content.thinking:
The user has confirmed:
1. **Approach**: Matchlock-based - adapt Matchlock's VM runtime as a library
2. **Lifecycle**: Per-workspace - one sandbox shared by all extensions, created on first Executor call, destroyed on workspace close

Now I have enough information to design the plan. Let me think about what this looks like:

## Architecture Design

### Core Components

1. **SandboxedExecutor** - Implements `workspaceapi.Executor` interface
   - Wraps a sandbox runtime
   - Manages sandbox lifecycle (lazy init, cleanup)
   - Routes `Start()` and `Signal()` calls into sandbox

2. **Sandbox Runtime** - Manages VM lifecycle
   - Based on Matchlock's infrastructure
   - Platform-specific backends (Firecracker for Linux, Virtualization.framework for macOS)
   - Guest agent communication over vsock

3. **Guest Agent** - Runs inside sandbox VM
   - Receives command execution requests
   - Handles I/O streaming
   - Process management and signal forwarding

4. **Policy Engine** - Enforces sandbox policies
   - Network allowlisting
   - Filesystem restrictions
   - Resource limits (CPU, memory, disk)

### Package Structure

```
api/sandboxapi/
├── sandbox.go            # Core types and interfaces
├── executor.go           # SandboxedExecutor implementation
├── policy.go             # Policy definitions
├── runtime/
│   ├── runtime.go        # Runtime interface
│   ├── matchlock/        # Matchlock-based implementation
│   │   ├── runtime.go
│   │   └── guest/        # Guest agent code
│   └── native/           # Native fallback (no VM)
└── sandboxtest/
    └── mock.go
```

### Integration Flow

1. Rune creates a `SandboxedExecutor` with workspace config
2. Extension calls `executor.Start(ctx, cmd)`


3. SandboxedExecutor checks if sandbox is running for this workspace, spawning the VM via Matchlock if needed, then mounts the workspace directory and sends the command to the guest agent over vsock, streaming I/O back to the Cmd's readers/writers. On workspace close, the sandbox VM tears down.

### Matchlock Integration Strategy

Looking at Matchlock's architecture, it exposes a CLI (`matchlock sandbox start/attach/stop`) and Go SDK at `github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock/pkg/sdk` with guest agent communication over vsock. I need to evaluate whether to wrap the CLI or use the SDK directly.

For a production IDE, vendoring or forking Matchlock makes more sense than importing it as a dependency—this gives us control over the codebase, lets us adapt it to our Executor interface, and removes reliance on external package stability. We'd create a new `api/sandboxapi/` package as the integration point, have Rune host use `SandboxedExecutor` when sandbox is enabled, and define sandbox policy and configuration settings. Now I'll map out the detailed implementation plan.

content.text:

- **Approach**: Matchlock-based VM sandbox
- **Lifecycle**: Per-workspace (shared by extensions, lazy init, cleanup on workspace close)
- **Integration**: Transparent `workspaceapi.Executor` wrapper

Let me now launch a Plan agent to design the detailed implementation, then write the final plan.

input.prompt:
Design a detailed implementation plan for a sandboxed Executor that implements the `workspaceapi.Executor` interface transparently.

## Context

The Rune Go SDK needs a sandbox implementation where:
1. Extensions call `executor.Start(ctx, cmd)` normally
2. The command runs inside a sandbox VM (not on the host)
3. Stdin/stdout/stderr stream back transparently
4. Signal() calls forward to the sandboxed process

## Requirements

- **Platform support**: Linux x86_64, Linux arm64, macOS arm64 (macOS x86_64 low priority)
- **Technology**: Based on Matchlock (github.com/jingkaihe/matchlock) which uses:
  - Firecracker microVMs on Linux
  - Apple Virtualization.framework on macOS
- **Lifecycle**: One sandbox per workspace, lazy-initialized on first Executor call, destroyed on workspace close
- **Transparent**: Callers shouldn't know they're using a sandbox

## workspaceapi.Executor Interface (from workspace.go)

```go
type Executor interface {
    Start(context.Context, *Cmd) error
    Signal(Pid, syscall.Signal) error
}

type Cmd struct {
    Name string           // Executable path
    Dir string            // Working directory  
    Args []string         // Arguments
    Env []string          // Environment variables
    Stdin io.Reader
    Stdout io.Writer
    Stderr io.Writer
    SysProcAttr *syscall.SysProcAttr
    ProcessWatcher        // Callback interface
}
```

## Sandbox Features Needed (from user requirements)

1. **Process Isolation**: VM-level isolation via Firecracker/Virtualization.framework
2. **Resource Controls**: CPU, memory, disk limits via cgroups (Linux) / VM config
3. **Network Policy**: Default-deny egress, domain allowlisting, no listening sockets
4. **Filesystem**: Copy-on-write overlay, workspace mounted via FUSE/vsock
5. **Secret Protection**: Environment scrubbing, credential injection via proxy
6. **Audit Trail**: Log all commands, file operations, network requests
7. **Rollback**: Ephemeral filesystem, changes reviewable before commit

## Design Tasks

1. Define the `SandboxedExecutor` type that wraps a sandbox runtime
2. Design the sandbox lifecycle management (init, exec, cleanup)
3. Design the guest agent protocol for command execution over vsock
4. Design policy configuration (network rules, resource limits, allowed paths)
5. Define how workspace files are mounted into the sandbox
6. Design signal forwarding mechanism
7. Plan the Matchlock integration (vendor, fork, or depend)
8. Define the package structure following existing api/ patterns

## Existing Patterns to Follow

- API packages use: `api/<name>/<name>.go` for interfaces, `<name>rpc/` for proto/gRPC
- Builder pattern for configuration
- Table-driven tests
- 90-column line limit
- Apache 2.0 license headers

Provide a comprehensive implementation plan with specific types, methods, and package organization.

1→// Copyright 2026 Unstable Build, LLC.
     2→//

    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package workspaceapi
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"io"
    20→	"os"
    21→	"syscall"
    22→)
    23→
    24→// Pid is an Executor's command identifier. It doesn't necessarily translate
    25→// to an os.Process.Pid.
    26→type Pid int
    27→
    28→// FileSystem abstracts the public facing API of a workspace file system.
    29→type FileSystem interface {
    30→	URI(path string) (URI, error)
    31→
    32→	// OpenFile opens a file at path with the given flag and mode.
    33→	OpenFile(path string, flag int, mode os.FileMode) (File, error)
    34→
    35→	// Remove removes the file at path.
    36→	Remove(path string) error
    37→
    38→	// Stat returns a FileInfo describing the named file.
    39→	Stat(path string) (os.FileInfo, error)
    40→
    41→	// ReadDir reads the named directory, returning all its directory entries.
    42→	ReadDir(name string) ([]os.DirEntry, error)
    43→
    44→	// MkdirAll creates a directory named path, along with any necessary parents,
    45→	// and returns nil, or else returns an error. The permission bits perm (before
    46→	// umask) are used for all directories that MkdirAll creates. If path is
    47→	// already a directory, MkdirAll does nothing and returns nil.
    48→	MkdirAll(path string, perm os.FileMode) error
    49→}
    50→
    51→// Cmd represents an external command being prepared to run. See exec.Cmd for
    52→// more details. Stdin, Stderr and Stdout, if set, will have their corresponding
    53→type Cmd struct {
    54→	Path    string
    55→	Dir     string
    56→	Args    []string
    57→	Env     []string
    58→	Stdin   io.Reader
    59→	Stdout  io.Writer
    60→	Stderr  io.Writer
    61→	Watcher ProcessWatcher
    62→
    63→	// SysProcAttr is ignored if passed from a extension.
    64→	SysProcAttr *syscall.SysProcAttr
    65→}
    66→
    67→// ProcessWatcher adds the ability for wait a processes started by Cmd
    68→// and collect any I/O errors or non-zero exit code.
    69→type ProcessWatcher interface {
    70→	// WatchProcess returns a channel that can be used to
    71→	// wait for the command to exit and waits for any copying to stdin or
    72→	// copying from stdout or stderr to complete.
    73→
    74→	// The returned error is nil if the command runs, has no problems copying
    75→	// stdin, stdout, and stderr, and exits with a zero exit status.
    76→	WatchProcess() chan error
    77→}
    78→
    79→// Executor is the public facing API of a workspace's command execution.
    80→type Executor interface {
    81→	// Start starts the given cmd and returns the Pid of the underlying
    82→	// process.
    83→	Start(context.Context, Cmd) (Pid, error)
    84→
    85→	// Signal sends a signal to the running process.
    86→	Signal(Pid, syscall.Signal) error
    87→
    88→	io.Closer
    89→}

   104→	Master File
